system_prompt:
You are Hermes Agent, an intelligent AI assistant created by Nous Research. You are helpful, knowledgeable, and direct. You assist users with a wide range of tasks including answering questions, writing and editing code, analyzing information, creative work, and executing actions via your tools. You communicate clearly, admit uncertainty when appropriate, and prioritize being genuinely useful over being verbose unless otherwise directed below. Be targeted and efficient in your exploration and investigations.

Conversation started: Sunday, April 12, 2026 12:58 AM
Model: anthropic/claude-opus-4.6
Provider: openrouter

You are on a text messaging communication platform, Telegram. Please do not use markdown as it does not render. You can send media files natively: to deliver a file to the user, include MEDIA:/absolute/path/to/file in your response. Images (.png, .jpg, .webp) appear as photos, audio (.ogg) sends as voice bubbles, and videos (.mp4) play inline. You can also include image URLs in markdown format ![alt](url) and they will be sent as native photos.

function.name: patch
function.description:
Targeted find-and-replace edits in files. Use this instead of sed/awk in terminal. Uses fuzzy matching (9 strategies) so minor whitespace/indentation differences won't break it. Returns a unified diff. Auto-runs syntax checks after editing.

Replace mode (default): find a unique string and replace it.
Patch mode: apply V4A multi-file patches for bulk changes.

mode.description: Edit mode: 'replace' for targeted find-and-replace, 'patch' for V4A multi-file patches

path.description: File path to edit (required for 'replace' mode)

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'replace' mode). Must be unique in the file unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'replace' mode). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: Replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false)

patch.description:
V4A format patch content (required for 'patch' mode). Format:
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: path/to/file
@@ context hint @@
 context line
-removed line
+added line
*** End Patch

function.name: process
function.description: Manage background processes started with terminal(background=true). Actions: 'list' (show all), 'poll' (check status + new output), 'log' (full output with pagination), 'wait' (block until done or timeout), 'kill' (terminate), 'write' (send raw stdin data without newline), 'submit' (send data + Enter, for answering prompts).

action.description: Action to perform on background processes

session_id.description: Process session ID (from terminal background output). Required for all actions except 'list'.

data.description: Text to send to process stdin (for 'write' and 'submit' actions)

timeout.description: Max seconds to block for 'wait' action. Returns partial output on timeout.

offset.description: Line offset for 'log' action (default: last 200 lines)

limit.description: Max lines to return for 'log' action

function.name: read_file
function.description: Read a text file with line numbers and pagination. Use this instead of cat/head/tail in terminal. Output format: 'LINE_NUM|CONTENT'. Suggests similar filenames if not found. Use offset and limit for large files. Reads exceeding ~100K characters are rejected; use offset and limit to read specific sections of large files. NOTE: Cannot read images or binary files — use vision_analyze for images.

path.description: Path to the file to read (absolute, relative, or ~/path)

offset.description: Line number to start reading from (1-indexed, default: 1)

limit.description: Maximum number of lines to read (default: 500, max: 2000)

function.name: search_files
function.description:
Search file contents or find files by name. Use this instead of grep/rg/find/ls in terminal. Ripgrep-backed, faster than shell equivalents.

Content search (target='content'): Regex search inside files. Output modes: full matches with line numbers, file paths only, or match counts.

File search (target='files'): Find files by glob pattern (e.g., '*.py', '*config*'). Also use this instead of ls — results sorted by modification time.

pattern.description: Regex pattern for content search, or glob pattern (e.g., '*.py') for file search

target.description: 'content' searches inside file contents, 'files' searches for files by name

path.description: Directory or file to search in (default: current working directory)

file_glob.description: Filter files by pattern in grep mode (e.g., '*.py' to only search Python files)

limit.description: Maximum number of results to return (default: 50)

offset.description: Skip first N results for pagination (default: 0)

output_mode.description: Output format for grep mode: 'content' shows matching lines with line numbers, 'files_only' lists file paths, 'count' shows match counts per file

context.description: Number of context lines before and after each match (grep mode only)

function.name: terminal
function.description:
Execute shell commands on a Linux environment. Filesystem usually persists between calls.

Do NOT use cat/head/tail to read files — use read_file instead.
Do NOT use grep/rg/find to search — use search_files instead.
Do NOT use ls to list directories — use search_files(target='files') instead.
Do NOT use sed/awk to edit files — use patch instead.
Do NOT use echo/cat heredoc to create files — use write_file instead.
Reserve terminal for: builds, installs, git, processes, scripts, network, package managers, and anything that needs a shell.

Foreground (default): Commands return INSTANTLY when done, even if the timeout is high. Set timeout=300 for long builds/scripts — you'll still get the result in seconds if it's fast. Prefer foreground for short commands.
Background: Set background=true to get a session_id. Two patterns:
  (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers).
  (2) Long-running tasks with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working on other things and the system auto-notifies you when the task finishes. Great for test suites, builds, deployments, or anything that takes more than a minute.
Use process(action="poll") for progress checks, process(action="wait") to block until done.
Working directory: Use 'workdir' for per-command cwd.
PTY mode: Set pty=true for interactive CLI tools (Codex, Claude Code, Python REPL).

Do NOT use vim/nano/interactive tools without pty=true — they hang without a pseudo-terminal. Pipe git output to cat if it might page.
Important: cloud sandboxes may be cleaned up, idled out, or recreated between turns. Persistent filesystem means files can resume later; it does NOT guarantee a continuously running machine or surviving background processes. Use terminal sandboxes for task work, not durable hosting.

command.description: The command to execute on the VM

background.description: Run the command in the background. Two patterns: (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers). (2) Long-running tasks paired with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working and get notified when the task finishes. For short commands, prefer foreground with a generous timeout instead.

timeout.description: Max seconds to wait (default: 180). Returns INSTANTLY when command finishes — set high for long tasks, you won't wait unnecessarily.

workdir.description: Working directory for this command (absolute path). Defaults to the session working directory.

check_interval.description: Seconds between automatic status checks for background processes (gateway/messaging only, minimum 30). When set, I'll proactively report progress.

pty.description: Run in pseudo-terminal (PTY) mode for interactive CLI tools like Codex, Claude Code, or Python REPL. Only works with local and SSH backends. Default: false.

notify_on_complete.description: When true (and background=true), you'll be automatically notified when the process finishes — no polling needed. Use this for tasks that take a while (tests, builds, deployments) so you can keep working on other things in the meantime.

function.name: write_file
function.description: Write content to a file, completely replacing existing content. Use this instead of echo/cat heredoc in terminal. Creates parent directories automatically. OVERWRITES the entire file — use 'patch' for targeted edits.

path.description: Path to the file to write (will be created if it doesn't exist, overwritten if it does)

content.description: Complete content to write to the file

[Subdirectory context discovered: /opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/AGENTS.md]
# Law Firm Wiki — Compiler Schema

> This file is the specification for the LLM that compiles activity logs
> into structured knowledge articles. Read it in full before any compile
> or query operation.

## What You Are

You are a knowledge compiler for a personal injury law firm. Your job is
to read raw case activity logs and extract reusable institutional knowledge
— the process, pitfalls, patterns, and best practices that make a PI
practice work.

You are NOT creating a case database. You are building a **wiki** — a
structured collection of concept articles about HOW PI cases work, derived
from evidence in real cases.

## The Three Operations

### 1. COMPILE (ingest raw → update wiki)

Read activity logs and case files. For each meaningful pattern you identify:

1. Check `index.md` — does a concept article already exist?
2. If yes → UPDATE it: add new evidence, adjust confidence, update Related
3. If no → CREATE it: full article per the Article Schema below
4. If two concepts interact → create/update a Connection article
5. Update `index.md` with any new or modified articles
6. Append a summary to `log.md`

**What to extract:**
- Process patterns (what happens and in what order)
- Decision points (why certain choices were made)
- Problems and resolutions (what went wrong, how it was fixed)
- Timeline patterns (how long things actually take)
- Communication patterns (what works with adjusters, clients, providers)
- Edge cases (unusual situations and how they were handled)
- Anti-patterns (things that caused problems)

**What to IGNORE:**
- Individual client PII (names, SSN, DOB, addresses, phone numbers)
- Specific dollar amounts tied to identifiable cases
- Attorney-client privileged communications
- Raw email addresses and phone numbers
- Replace specifics with anonymized references: "Case A", "Case B", or
  descriptive patterns: "in cases involving [treatment gap > 30 days]"

**Confidence scoring:**
- high: Pattern observed in 5+ cases with consistent outcomes
- medium: Pattern observed in 2-4 cases
- low: Single case observation, or conflicting evidence

### 2. QUERY (ask questions → synthesize answers)

1. Read `index.md` to identify 3-10 relevant concept articles
2. Read those articles in full
3. Synthesize an answer with [[wikilink]] citations
4. If the answer reveals a gap → note it in the answer
5. Optionally file the Q&A back into `wiki/qa/`

### 3. LINT (health check)

Run these checks and write results to `wiki/reports/`:
- **Broken wikilinks**: Links that point to non-existent articles
- **Orphan articles**: Concepts with zero inbound links
- **Phase coverage**: Which PHASE_DAG phases have sparse knowledge
- **Stale articles**: Source data changed since last compile
- **Contradictions**: Articles that contain conflicting guidance

## Article Schema

### Concept Article (wiki/concepts/*.md)

```yaml
---
title: Human-Readable Title
slug: kebab-case-slug
phase: [phase_0_onboarding]    # which PHASE_DAG phases this applies to
category: one-of-below
tags: [tag1, tag2, tag3]
confidence: high               # high/medium/low
evidence_count: 5              # number of cases with supporting evidence
created: YYYY-MM-DD
updated: YYYY-MM-DD
related:                       # other concept slugs
  - related-concept-one
  - related-concept-two
---

# Title

> One-paragraph summary (2-3 sentences max)

## Pattern

Detailed explanation of the pattern, process, or concept.

## Evidence

Anonymized observations from source cases:
- "In N cases where [condition], [outcome]"
- "When [action was taken], [result]"

## Agent Guidance

Actionable instructions for paralegal agents:
1. Step one
2. Step two
3. Step three

## Pitfalls

- Common mistake one
- Common mistake two

## Related

- [[related-concept-one]] — how it connects
- [[related-concept-two]] — how it connects
```

### Categories

- `process`: Standard workflows and procedures
- `adjuster-tactics`: Insurance company behavior patterns
- `edge-cases`: Unusual situations requiring special handling
- `phase-knowledge`: Phase-specific best practices
- `communication`: Client/adjuster/provider communication patterns
- `financial`: Billing, liens, settlements, funding
- `medical`: Treatment patterns, records, chronologies
- `legal`: Litigation, discovery, depositions, trial
- `tools`: Software, systems, and process tools

### Connection Article (wiki/connections/*.md)

Same schema but `category: connection` and the body explains how
two or more concepts interact. Example:

```
treatment-gaps-affect-demand-value.md
→ Links: treatment-gap-defense + demand-timing-strategy
→ Insight: Cases with documented gap explanations recovered 80% of
  expected value vs 50% for unexplained gaps
```

### Q&A Article (wiki/qa/*.md)

```yaml
---
title: Question text
slug: question-slug
date: YYYY-MM-DD
sources:                       # concept articles consulted
  - concept-slug-one
  - concept-slug-two
---

# Question

[Full question text]

## Answer

[Synthesized answer with [[wikilink]] citations]

## Gaps Identified

[Any knowledge gaps the question revealed]
```

## Index Schema (wiki/index.md)

The master catalog. This is the LLM's entry point for all queries.

```markdown
# Law Firm Wiki — Index

Last compiled: YYYY-MM-DD | Articles: N | Connections: N

## By Phase

### Phase 0: Onboarding
| Article | Category | Confidence | Evidence |
|---------|----------|------------|----------|
| [[concepts/onboarding-pitfalls]] | phase-knowledge | high | 15 |

### Phase 1: File Setup
...

## By Category

### Adjuster Tactics
| Article | Phase | Confidence |
|---------|-------|------------|
| [[concepts/treatment-gap-defense]] | 2,3 | high |
...

## Recent Updates

| Date | Article | Action |
|------|---------|--------|
| 2026-04-12 | treatment-gap-defense | created |
```

## Activity Log Format Reference

Activity logs live at `cases/<slug>/Activity Log/*.md` and have this structure:

```yaml
---
schema_version: 2
case_id: uuid
date: "YYYY-MM-DD"
time: "HH:MM:SS"         # optional
category: legal|correspondence|imported|financial|medical|task
subcategory: email|filevine_negotiation|outbound_call|...
---

# Category — Date

**Case:** [[cases/slug/slug|Client Name]]
**Source:** description

[Free-form content: emails, notes, call summaries, task descriptions]
```

### Subcategory Distribution (21K+ logs)
- email: 3,078
- filevine_negotiation: 1,620
- outbound_call: 890
- missed_call: 780
- filevine_treatment: 717
- filevine_demandinprogress: 645
- demand: 534
- filevine_filesetup: 369
- phase_change: 254
- filevine_uim: 179
- inbound_call: 137
- esignature: 78
- call_summary: 10

## PHASE_DAG Reference

The case lifecycle has 8 phases:
- Phase 0: Onboarding (contract, HIPAA, intake)
- Phase 1: File Setup (insurance claims, provider setup)
- Phase 2: Treatment (monitoring, records collection)
- Phase 3: Demand (drafting, review, sending)
- Phase 4: Negotiation (offers, counters, strategy)
- Phase 5: Settlement (agreement, disbursement)
- Phase 6: Lien Resolution (PIP waterfall, lien negotiation)
- Phase 7: Litigation (complaint, discovery, trial)
- Phase 8: Closed

## Rules

1. NEVER include identifiable client information in wiki articles
2. Anonymize all case references (use "Case A", "Case B", or descriptive patterns)
3. Every article must have at least one [[wikilink]] to a related concept
4. Update index.md whenever you create or modify an article
5. Append to log.md whenever you compile
6. Confidence scores must reflect actual evidence count
7. When in doubt about a pattern's validity, use confidence: low
8. Prefer specific actionable guidance over vague advice
9. Group knowledge by PHASE_DAG phase when possible
10. Flag contradictions explicitly — don't silently resolve them

[Subdirectory context discovered: /opt/data/FirmVault/CLAUDE.md]
# FirmVault — Agent Orientation

> **Read this in full at the start of every session.** It names the thesis, the non-negotiable rules, and where to find the authoritative docs. Short deliberately — use the imports below for detail.

## What this repo is

FirmVault is the **development environment for an AI paralegal backend** intended to eventually be sold to other personal-injury law firms. This specific repo is Whaley Law Firm's own instance; the architecture we prove here becomes the product.

Two parallel product tracks exist:

1. **This repo** — the GitHub-native paralegal backend: markdown vault, `PHASE_DAG.yaml`, `SKILL.md` library, runtime (materializer + worker), intake pipeline.
2. **Separate Huly fork** (not in this repo) — the lawyer-facing UI. This repo's "UI" is for operators, not lawyers.

## The thesis

**"The firm as a code repo."** Law firm work is repeatable tasks + templates + rules, which is exactly what code is. Put the firm's work into primitives agents already excel at (issues, branches, PRs, diffs) and you get a much better AI experience than any traditional PMS bolt-on.

## Core architectural principles

1. **The vault is a shadow, not the source of truth.** Real files (signed PDFs, audio, emails, faxes) live on the firm's own storage (their server, Dropbox, Box, Drive). The markdown in `cases/<slug>/` is a PHI-masked projection of those files.
2. **Intake is a pipeline, not an agent**: document arrives → real file placed on user's storage → converted to markdown → PHI stripped → opens a PR against this repo. The PR is the first human-in-the-loop gate.
3. **Git is the deliberate interface.** Every agent action is a commit or PR. Free audit trail, free approval gates, trivial rollback. Agents *never* touch real files — only their masked shadows.
4. **Markdown, not a graph DB.** We tried FalkorDB and abandoned it. LLM agents are empirically more reliable on plain text + grep than on Cypher queries. This is a deliberate choice, not laziness.
5. **PHI never lives in the vault.** Placeholders like `{{client_ssn}}` and `{{dob}}` reference secrets held in the user's own infrastructure. At render time, deterministic code (not the model) substitutes real values. The model never sees raw PII.
6. **The vault is the only state store for this layer.** No external database. No per-case JSON files. No FalkorDB. No `${ROSCOE_ROOT}`. See `@skills.tools.workflows/DATA_CONTRACT.md` for the authoritative mapping.

## Do NOT do these things

These are actively deprecated. If you see them in old code or docs, treat them as bugs.

- **Do not reference FalkorDB, Cypher, `graph_client`, `graph_manager`, or `run_cypher_query`.** All removed. Everything reads the vault.
- **Do not reference per-case JSON files** (`overview.json`, `contacts.json`, `insurance.json`, `liens.json`, `expenses.json`, `medical_providers.json`, `case_state.json`). Deleted.
- **Do not reference `${ROSCOE_ROOT}`** or any absolute-path scaffolding. Everything is repo-relative.
- **Do not edit content between `<!-- roscoe-medical-start -->` / `<!-- roscoe-medical-end -->` or `<!-- roscoe-insurance-start -->` / `<!-- roscoe-insurance-end -->` markers.** Those blocks are owned by the import scripts and will be overwritten on next sync.
- **Do not invent new vault paths.** If the thing you want to write doesn't have a home in `DATA_CONTRACT.md`, update the contract first (same commit), then write. Drift between contract and reality is the bug.
- **Do not create top-level dashboards, reports, or state files outside the vault layout**. Operator views read the vault via GitHub API or git; they don't create parallel state.

## Where the authoritative docs live

Import these at launch — they are the contract, not me paraphrasing:

@DESIGN.md

@skills.tools.workflows/DATA_CONTRACT.md

@MEMORY.md

Also non-imported but worth knowing:

- `skills.tools.workflows/workflows/PHASE_DAG.yaml` — machine-readable phase definitions, landmarks, and predicate vocabulary. The materializer reads this.
- `skills.tools.workflows/runtime/README.md` — how the materializer and worker agents drive the task queue.
- `skills.tools.workflows/runtime/task_schema.md` — the contract for task issues (labels, frontmatter, lifecycle).
- `Templates/INDEX.md` — the firm template library (73 slugified templates grouped by category).
- `skills.tools.workflows/Skills/*/SKILL.md` — 42 modernized skills in Anthropic Agent Skills format. Exemplars: `lien-management`, `deposition-prep`, `deposition-strategy`, `pip-application`.

## Slug rules (critical for wikilinks)

From `DATA_CONTRACT.md` §4:

- Lowercase
- Apostrophes and curly quotes **stripped** (no replacement char): `K'daya` → `kdaya`
- `&` → `and`
- All other non-alphanumeric runs collapsed to a single hyphen
- Leading/trailing hyphens stripped

Mismatched slugs break wikilinks across the vault. Slug before you write.

## Runtime model (what's being built)

The system is being turned from passive documentation into an active work-driving loop:

```
Vault (cases/*) ← materializer (cron) → GitHub Issues ← worker (event) → Vault
```

- **Materializer** walks open cases, reads each case's `landmarks:` frontmatter + `PHASE_DAG.yaml`, opens a GitHub Issue for every unsatisfied landmark whose task template preconditions are met. Already prototyped as a prompt in `runtime/materializer_prompt.md`.
- **Worker** is triggered by the `status:ready` label on an issue, claims it, loads the named skill, runs it, commits vault changes that reference the task_id, and flips the issue status. Prototyped in `runtime/worker_prompt.md`.
- **Source of truth** for whether a landmark is satisfied is `case.frontmatter.landmarks.<id>` — the materializer checks this first and only falls back to evaluating a predicate if the key is missing.

## Working branch

All in-progress work lives on `claude/import-cases-from-drive-xiQgL`. Do not push directly to `main` without an explicit request.

## When in doubt

- Read `DATA_CONTRACT.md` before writing to the vault.
- Read `PHASE_DAG.yaml` before touching workflow/landmark logic.
- Read `MEMORY.md` to find out what's in flight and what's pending.
- Check the last 3–5 commits on the working branch to see the most recent decisions.

The space in "Activity Log" is causing path splitting issues. Let me use proper quoting with a find-based approach.

function.name: terminal
function.arguments:
{"command": "cd /opt/data/FirmVault
# Use find to handle spaces in "Activity Log"
for slug in estate-of-evangeline-young charles-t-johnson colleen-colvin daniel-w-volk; do
  echo "========== $slug =========="
  files=($(find "cases/$slug/Activity Log" -name "*.md" | sort))
  total=${#files[@]}
  echo "Total logs: $total"
  
  # First 5
  for i in 0 1 2 3 4; do
    if [ $i -lt $total ]; then
      echo "--- FIRST[$i]: $(basename "${files[$i]}") ---"
      head -30 "${files[$i]}"
    fi
  done
  
  # Last 5
  for i in $(seq $((total-5)) $((total-1))); do
    if [ $i -ge 0 ] && [ $i -lt $total ]; then
      echo "--- LAST[$i]: $(basename "${files[$i]}") ---"
      head -30 "${files[$i]}"
    fi
  done
done", "timeout": 60}

function.name: terminal
function.arguments:
{"command": "cd /opt/data/FirmVault
# Get mid-case negotiation/litigation patterns from each case
for slug in colleen-colvin dana-jackson daniel-w-volk james-kiper; do
  echo "========== $slug - MID CASE SAMPLES =========="
  find "cases/$slug/Activity Log" -name "*negotiation*" -o -name "*demand*" -o -name "*litigation*" 2>/dev/null | sort | head -5 | while IFS= read -r f; do
    echo "--- $(basename "$f") ---"
    head -25 "$f"
  done
  # Also get some correspondence and phase changes
  find "cases/$slug/Activity Log" -name "*phase_change*" -o -name "*task*" 2>/dev/null | sort | head -3 | while IFS= read -r f; do
    echo "--- $(basename "$f") ---"
    head -25 "$f"
  done
done", "timeout": 30}

    19|# Wrongful Death Case Patterns
    20|
    21|> Wrongful death cases differ fundamentally from standard PI cases because the injured party is deceased. The case is brought by a representative (typically a surviving family member) on behalf of the estate. These cases involve no medical treatment records (the decedent was pronounced dead at the scene), but require extensive investigation, accident reconstruction, vocational/economic expert evaluations, and often multi-carrier coordination when multiple at-fault parties or vehicles are involved.
    22|
    23|## Pattern
    24|
    25|### Key Differences from Standard PI Cases
    26|1. **Client is a representative**: The person the firm communicates with is not the injured party but a surviving family member acting on behalf of the estate
    27|2. **No medical treatment phase**: The decedent has no ongoing treatment, so the case skips the treatment phase entirely and moves from file setup into investigation and litigation
    28|3. **Investigation-heavy**: Wrongful death cases require significant investment in investigation (accident reconstruction, witness interviews, scene evidence) — costs can exceed $20K
    29|4. **Expert evaluations focus on lost earnings**: Vocational economics experts evaluate the decedent's earning capacity and life expectancy rather than treatment outcomes
    30|5. **Multi-carrier complexity**: When multiple vehicles or entities are involved, separate BI claims are filed against each carrier, creating parallel negotiation tracks
    31|6. **Higher litigation costs**: Court reporting, investigation, accident reconstruction, and expert fees are substantially higher than typical PI cases
    32|7. **Settlement release coordination**: Release negotiations involve multiple defense counsel firms and can span months of back-and-forth correspondence
    33|8. **Medicare considerations**: Even in death cases, Medicare questions may arise during settlement if the decedent had Medicare coverage
    34|
    35|### Wrongful Death Case Lifecycle
    36|- Intake via representative (surviving family member)
    37|- Insurance claim setup (multiple carriers if applicable)
    38|- Investigation and evidence collection (police reports, accident reconstruction)
    39|- Expert retention (vocational economics, accident reconstruction)
    40|- Complaint filing and litigation
    41|- Discovery and depositions (of representative, witnesses)
    42|- Settlement negotiations (potentially with multiple defense counsel)
    43|- Release execution and disbursement
    44|
    45|## Evidence
    46|
    47|- In a wrongful death case (DOI 2/2022, KY jurisdiction), the decedent was pronounced dead at the scene of a multi-vehicle crash. A surviving family member served as representative and primary contact. The case involved three separate BI carriers (a personal auto carrier, a commercial trucking company's national liabilities office, and a third personal auto carrier), each requiring independent LOR filing and claim setup. Investigation costs exceeded $20K (focus investigation services). Accident reconstruction ($2K) and vocational economics evaluation ($3.2K) were retained. The case was filed in circuit court (Marshall County 23-CI-00073). Court reporter costs across depositions exceeded $1.1K. Additional litigation expenses included multiple defense law firms ($570, $688, $2K, $2.1K). Settlement release negotiations extended over months with back-and-forth emails between the firm and defense counsel. A Medicare question arose during settlement, requiring coordination. The total case timeline exceeded 3 years from DOI with resolution still pending. Pre-settlement funding ($4K) and workers' compensation lien ($171K+) were also present, adding significant lien complexity. A notary service was used to obtain the representative's signature remotely.
    48|
    49|## Agent Guidance
    50|
    51|1. During intake, identify the representative and their relationship to the decedent — confirm legal authority to act on behalf of the estate
    52|2. Skip the treatment/records collection phase — focus immediately on investigation and insurance claim setup
    53|3. File LORs with all potentially liable carriers within the first week
    54|4. Budget for substantially higher case expenses: investigation ($10K-20K+), accident reconstruction, vocational economics, court reporting
    55|5. Coordinate with multiple defense counsel firms — each carrier typically retains separate counsel
    56|6. Track release negotiations carefully — multiple defense firms means multiple release documents
    57|7. Check for Medicare/Medicaid liens even in death cases — the decedent may have had coverage
    58|8. Maintain regular contact with the representative — they are grieving and need empathetic communication alongside case updates
    59|
    60|## Pitfalls
    61|
    62|- Treating a wrongful death case like a standard PI case (no treatment phase, investigation-focused)
    63|- Not identifying all potentially liable parties and their carriers early
    64|- Underbudgeting for investigation and expert costs
    65|- Not confirming the representative's legal authority to act for the estate
    66|- Allowing settlement release negotiations to stall without follow-up
    67|- Ignoring Medicare/lien considerations in death cases
    68|
    69|## Related
    70|
    71|- [[litigation-process-patterns]] — wrongful death cases are almost always litigated
    72|- [[multi-carrier-multi-defendant-cases]] — multi-vehicle wrongful death involves parallel carrier tracks
    73|- [[client-communication-through-family]] — the representative is always a family member
    74|- [[expert-reports-and-evaluations]] — vocational economics and accident reconstruction are critical
    75|- [[medicare-lien-management]] — Medicare questions can arise even in death cases
    76|", "total_lines": 75, "file_size": 6047, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

    16|---
    17|
    18|# Dog Bite Case Patterns
    19|
    20|> Dog bite cases follow a distinct pattern from auto accidents: liability depends on identifying the animal owner and their homeowner's insurance (not auto insurance), the client is frequently a minor (requiring guardian communication), and identifying insurance coverage can be a prolonged investigation. These cases may require creative investigation (address searches, certified mail to property/animal owners) and frequently proceed to litigation when the dog owner is uncooperative or uninsured.
    21|
    22|## Pattern
    23|
    24|### Liability Investigation
    25|Dog bite cases require identifying:
    26|1. **The animal owner** — may be different from the property owner where the attack occurred
    27|2. **The property owner** — relevant for property liability theories
    28|3. **Homeowner's insurance** — the primary coverage source (not auto insurance)
    29|
    30|Investigation tools include:
    31|- Address searches to identify residents at the animal's location
    32|- Certified mail to property/dog owners requesting insurance information (LOR with turnover letter)
    33|- Return receipt tracking (green cards) — if unclaimed, must resend to alternate addresses
    34|- Direct contact with identified parties to request homeowner's insurance card
    35|- Video evidence from home security cameras (e.g., Vivint) obtained from neighbors
    36|
    37|### Insurance Identification Challenge
    38|Unlike auto accidents where insurance is identified through the police report, dog bite cases require the firm to independently identify insurance:
    39|- Send LOR/turnover letter to animal owner via certified mail
    40|- Allow 30 days for defendant to provide insurance information
    41|- If no response, investigate property owner (who may be different from animal owner)
    42|- Homeowner's insurance may not be immediately identifiable — multiple attempts may be needed
    43|
    44|### Minor Client Considerations
    45|Dog bite clients are frequently minors, creating special requirements:
    46|- Guardian (typically parent) is the primary point of contact
    47|- All communication goes through the guardian
    48|- Settlement of a minor's claim may require court approval
    49|- Both guardian and minor may need to be deposed in litigation
    50|
    51|### Treatment Pattern
    52|- ER visit for initial treatment (wound care, infection check)
    53|- Follow-up with PCP/pediatrician within days
    54|- Monitoring for infection/inflammation
    55|- Treatment is often brief compared to auto accident cases (1-2 provider visits)
    56|
    57|## Evidence
    58|
    59|- In a dog bite case involving a minor (DOI 8/2022), the firm identified the animal's location (adjacent property), performed an address search to identify residents, and sent certified mail LOR to the identified occupants. The first certified letter was unclaimed and returned; a second letter was signed by a defendant. After 30+ days with no insurance response, the firm texted the defendant directly requesting a photo of their homeowner's insurance card. Insurance was eventually identified as a regional farm bureau carrier. The guardian provided video evidence from a neighbor's home security system showing the animal running loose and attacking the client. The case moved to litigation when settlement appeared insufficient. Depositions of both guardian and minor were scheduled, cancelled by defense counsel, then rescheduled. A jury trial date was ultimately set. The case spanned 3+ years from DOI to ongoing litigation.
    60|
    61|## Agent Guidance
    62|
    63|1. At intake for dog bite cases, immediately confirm the animal's location address and identify all residents/property owners
    64|2. Perform address search to identify potential defendants
    65|3. Send LOR + turnover letter via certified mail to all identified parties
    66|4. Track certified mail via USPS — if unclaimed, attempt alternative addresses
    67|5. Allow 30 days for insurance response, then escalate (text, call, or investigate property owner separately)
    68|6. Request video/photo evidence from the guardian immediately (neighbors may have security cameras)
    69|7. Communicate exclusively through the guardian when the client is a minor
    70|8. Prepare for litigation: dog bite cases with uncooperative owners frequently require complaint filing
    71|9. When scheduling depositions for minor clients, coordinate with guardian for both depositions
    72|
    73|## Pitfalls
    74|
    75|- Assuming the animal owner has homeowner's insurance — they may be a renter without coverage
    76|- Not distinguishing between the animal owner and the property owner
    77|- Failing to preserve video evidence promptly — home security systems may overwrite footage
    78|- Not tracking certified mail return receipts — proof of service may be needed for litigation
    79|- Communicating with the minor client directly instead of through the guardian
    80|- Underestimating case value because injuries appear minor — scarring and emotional impact may be significant
    81|
    82|## Related
    83|
    84|- [[premises-liability-case-patterns]] — similar property-based liability analysis
    85|- [[client-communication-through-family]] — guardian communication is the norm in minor client cases
    86|- [[litigation-process-patterns]] — dog bite cases frequently enter litigation
    87|- [[intake-and-onboarding-workflow]] — modified intake process for non-auto cases
    88|", "total_lines": 87, "file_size": 5626, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

     7|confidence: high

     9|created: 2026-04-12

    12|  - premises-liability-case-patterns

    17|---
    18|
    19|# Litigation Process Patterns
    20|
    21|> When PI cases proceed to litigation, they enter a structured legal process involving complaint filing, discovery (interrogatories, document production), depositions, motions practice, and potential mediation or trial. Litigation dramatically increases case complexity, timeline, and administrative burden on the paralegal team.
    22|
    23|## Pattern
    24|
    25|The litigation lifecycle follows a predictable sequence:
    26|
    27|### 1. Complaint Filing
    28|- Draft complaint with interrogatories (IROG)
    29|- File with the circuit court
    30|- Check service of process on defendant
    31|- Court assigns case number and division
    32|
    33|### 2. Discovery Phase
    34|- **Interrogatories**: Written questions exchanged between parties
    35|- **Supplemental responses**: As new information becomes available, supplemental discovery responses must be filed
    36|- **Document production**: Medical records, tax returns, employment records
    37|- **Subpoenas**: Third-party records (e.g., bank records from Hometown Bank) obtained via subpoena
    38|- **Timeline**: Court sets discovery deadlines; joint motions to extend are common
    39|
    40|### 3. Motions Practice
    41|- Motions to compel (when opposing party doesn't respond to discovery)
    42|- Motions to withdraw counsel
    43|- Joint motions for deadline extensions
    44|- Motion hearings may be remote or rescheduled
    45|
    46|### 4. Mediation
    47|- Typically scheduled after discovery is substantially complete
    48|- Mediator selected through national organizations (e.g., NADN)
    49|- Both parties must agree on mediator and date
    50|- Half-day sessions are common; can be via Zoom
    51|- Mediation may be cancelled and rescheduled
    52|
    53|### 5. Expert Coordination
    54|- FCE and IME reports ordered during litigation
    55|- Expert invoices must be tracked and paid
    56|- Reports may need amendments as treatment progresses
    57|
    58|### Court Filing System
    59|- Kentucky courts use eFiling with automated NEF (Notice of Electronic Filing) and NCP notifications
    60|- Multiple attorneys and paralegals receive notifications simultaneously
    61|
    62|## Evidence
    63|
    64|- In a premises liability case in litigation (Knox Circuit Court), the litigation spanned 3+ years (2022-2025+). Activities included: complaint filing with IROG, checking service, receiving motions, joint deadline extension motions, mediation scheduling and cancellation, IME amendment requests, FCE scheduling and rescheduling, economist report ordering, deposition scheduling, subpoena responses, and supplemental discovery responses.
    65|- Motion hearings were rescheduled multiple times (from 1/20/22 to 2/14/22 via Facebook notice from clerk).
    66|- Certified records were required for litigation — regular copies insufficient. Certification forms had to be corrected (date range "04/2014 thru Current").
    67|- Mediation was scheduled through the National Academy of Distinguished Neutrals (NADN) with a retired judge mediator, coordinated with defense counsel schedules.
    68|- In Case G (MVA, Boone County), complaint filed ~1 year post-accident. Full litigation lifecycle including discovery exchanges with defense counsel (Richardson Law Group), deposition scheduling via Kentuckiana Reporters, and mediation via Zoom. Mediation failed — defense offered "last best & final" amounts that were inadequate. Zoom mediation required coordination with clients on technical setup (muting, call-in numbers). The case remained active post-mediation failure.
    69|- In Case H (MVA, Barren County), complaint filed in Barren Circuit Court (23-CI-00659). The defendant's insurer (Kentucky Farm Bureau) filed a parallel subrogation action in District Court. Depositions were scheduled via Kentuckiana Reporters. Mediation was being scheduled via the same service. Service of process required checking via alias — "Check for Service - Issue Alias is not perfected" — indicating the defendant was difficult to serve.
    70|- In Case I (MVA, Jefferson Circuit Court), complaint filed ~8 months post-accident (24-CI-005774) due to disputed liability (BI only accepting 25%). Discovery followed, with defense counsel reviewing dash cam video. Depositions were scheduled via Zoom for both the client and a witness (companion passenger). Client nearly missed deposition — required multiple urgent texts ("Cecil, you good?", "Cecil you ready?", "Cecil?") before responding. Deposition completed successfully. Defense counsel served interrogatories and additional discovery.
    71|- In Case F (WC case), litigation followed a different track — DWC (Department of Workers' Compensation) proceedings with BRC (Benefit Review Conferences) scheduled telephonically. Defense counsel (Pohl & Aubrey) exchanged correspondence about Form 106 requests and IME scheduling. This WC litigation model is distinct from civil circuit court litigation.
    72|- In Case F (auto accident, litigation), discovery involved interrogatories and requests for production exchanged between plaintiff and defendant. Defense counsel (Dilbeck & Myers) engaged actively in scheduling depositions. The defendant's deposition was scheduled via Zoom, with the plaintiff's deposition to follow immediately after. The firm communicated with the client via text and phone to prepare for deposition. Defense counsel's responses to production requests were tracked through email exchanges.
    73|- In Case G (auto accident, UIM litigation), a UIM complaint was filed separately against the client's own insurer (Allstate) in Fayette Circuit Court. The insurer filed an answer to the complaint. Discovery commenced with the plaintiff's first set of discovery. The UIM adjuster received a courtesy copy of the complaint. This demonstrates that UIM litigation follows a parallel track to BI claims.
    74|- In Case H (auto accident, litigation with stolen vehicle), the case (22-CI-002878) involved multiple defendants and defense counsel from different firms. Key litigation events included: defense counsel requesting and receiving a 60-day extension to answer the complaint while attempting to get the insurer to accept coverage; an Examination Under Oath (EUO) of the defendant regarding the stolen vehicle claim; deposition scheduling disputes where defense counsel was "adamant" about timing constraints; unilateral scheduling of depositions when counsel could not agree; and audio/video recording requests from the court. The case lasted 4+ years from DOI through ongoing litigation.
    75|- In a PL case (Jefferson Circuit Court, DOI 9/2023) transferred from prior counsel, complaint was filed on the same day as file setup due to SOL within 60 days. Multiple defendants were named (individual parties + a commercial carrier LLC). Service of process required coordination with an out-of-state sheriff (Floyd County, IN) with associated fees. Multiple defense counsel from separate law firms entered appearances. Records requests were sent to 15+ providers simultaneously with discovery. The case generated heavy correspondence including call summaries with defense counsel. Defense counsel coordinated motion practice within weeks of filing.
    76|- In a dog bite case (Jefferson Circuit Court, DOI 8/2022), the case involved a minor client with guardian as contact. Homeowner's insurance was identified through KY Farm Bureau. After the BI claim settled, a separate complaint was filed. Discovery, depositions (of both guardian and minor client), and defense counsel rescheduling occurred. Deposition was cancelled and rescheduled multiple times by defense counsel. A jury trial date and final pretrial conference were set. The court system generated NEF/NCP notifications through kycourts.net eFiling.
    77|- In an elderly MVA case (DOI 1/2022), the case had a prior $800 offer before the firm's involvement. EvenUP was used for case valuation. Despite extensive treatment (21+ providers including specialists), the demand was emailed to defense attorney during active litigation. The client's voicemail was full during case updates, creating communication challenges during litigation.
    78|- In a WC case (Ford Motor Company, DOI 5/2023), defense counsel scheduled client deposition in-person at their office and an IME. The client passed away (11/2/2025) before the deposition (scheduled 11/14/2025). Family member called to notify firm of client's death. The firm then notified defense counsel of the death. This represents a unique litigation event — client death during active WC proceedings requiring immediate procedural action.
    79|- In a PL case (retail slip-and-fall, McLean County 24-CI-00012), complaint was filed against three corporate defendants (retail chain, property management company, property owner). Defense counsel from a large firm actively engaged in email correspondence about case status. Mediation was conducted but was unsuccessful. The case continued through active litigation with ongoing records collection from 10+ medical providers. Court reporter and mediation expenses were tracked ($400 mediation, $308 court reporting). The multi-defendant PL structure with separate defense representation confirms the pattern that PL cases produce the most litigation-intensive posture.
    80|- In a wrongful death case (Marshall County 23-CI-00073), the estate representative (surviving family member) was the litigation contact. The case involved three separate BI carriers (two auto carriers + one commercial trucking carrier) and required extensive investigation ($20K+ in investigation costs), accident reconstruction ($2K), and vocational economics evaluation ($3.2K). Court reporter costs across multiple depositions exceeded $1.1K. Settlement release negotiations spanned months with back-and-forth emails. A Medicare question arose during settlement, requiring coordination with defense counsel. This confirms that wrongful death cases in litigation incur substantially higher expert/investigation costs.
    81|- In an MVA case (Floyd County, IN), complaint was filed as a multi-plaintiff action (companion cases from the same accident). Deposition scheduling involved coordination through court reporting services. The case progressed through discovery to mediation scheduling. Defense counsel correspondence was ongoing through early 2026. The case crossed state lines (KY firm filing in IN court), adding jurisdictional complexity.
    82|- In a WC estate case (KY DWC claim 202200007), the full WC litigation lifecycle was observed: complaint filing with IROG, initial denial (Motion to Dismiss for "no living person named"), Motion to Substitute Plaintiff, Forms 104/105 and 45-Day Disclosures, BRC hearings (repeatedly rescheduled over 3+ years), defense RFPD, multiple IMEs through Commonwealth IME, ALJ opinion, and WC Board opinion concurring. Total timeline: 5+ years. This confirms the WC administrative proceedings track as fundamentally different from circuit court litigation, with LMS as the filing system.
    83|- In a multi-defendant auto accident case in litigation (Jefferson Circuit Court, 21-CI-004985, DOI 1/2021), the litigation spanned 4+ years (2021-2025+). Activities included: complaint filing, interrogatory responses to defense counsel, deposition scheduling with defense counsel, State Farm motion to dismiss (AOC 280 Final Submission for Adjudication), certified medical records collection via ChartSwap, government open records requests for police body cam footage (via Axon/Evidence.com), multiple ORRs to LMPD, amended subpoenas (to Verizon and Metro PCS for phone records), Louisville Metro public records requests, forwarding evidence to defense counsel (Dinsmore, Smith & Hoskins), and locating witnesses (other vehicle passengers). This is the most complex litigation case observed, involving multiple defense counsel, government records, and electronic evidence. The 4+ year timeline from DOI to ongoing litigation confirms that multi-defendant cases with uninsured drivers dramatically extend case duration.
    84|- In Case M (MVA, multi-plaintiff litigation, Floyd County), when four plaintiffs' counsel could not agree on distribution of tendered policy limits, the case moved to litigation. All plaintiffs were joined in one lawsuit. Litigation activity included deposition scheduling via court reporters, extensive correspondence between plaintiff and defense counsel over deposition dates and mediation scheduling, and preparation for mediation. The deposition scheduling required multiple rounds of email coordination spanning weeks (Nov 2025-Feb 2026). This case demonstrates that litigation can be triggered not by low offers or coverage denial but by inter-plaintiff distribution disputes.
    85|- In Case N (premises liability estate, Jefferson Circuit Court 21-CI-003798, DOI 7/2020), litigation spanned 5+ years. The case included: complaint filing with IROG, checking service of process, defense discovery requests via e-filing (CourtNet) and email, plaintiff's responses to interrogatories and RFAs (drafted by paralegal, reviewed by attorney), defendant's notice of intent to subpoena third-party medical records from multiple providers, defendant's use of Dropbox to share obtained records, plaintiff's discovery requests to the defendant, defense discovery production responses, and ongoing correspondence. Court notifications were tracked via NEF/NCP electronic filing system. The case was referred out to specialist counsel at one point, then continued. This is the longest continuously active premises liability litigation observed (5+ years).
    86|
    87|## Agent Guidance
    88|
    89|1. Create a litigation calendar at filing with all deadlines from the court's scheduling order
    90|2. Track every court notification (NEF, NCP) and file appropriately
    91|3. Maintain a discovery checklist: what's been requested, what's been produced, what's outstanding
    92|4. For supplemental discovery responses, track what new information needs to be disclosed
    93|5. Coordinate expert scheduling well in advance — FCE and IME providers have limited availability
    94|6. When mediator sends a Zoom link or scheduling confirmation, immediately distribute to all parties
    95|7. Monitor motion hearing schedule — courts frequently reschedule
    96|8. Keep a running litigation expense tracker (expert fees, filing fees, mediator fees)
    97|
    98|## Pitfalls
    99|
   100|- Missing court deadlines (can result in sanctions or adverse rulings)
   101|- Not tracking supplemental discovery obligations
   102|- Allowing expert reports to become stale (e.g., new treatment occurred after IME)
   103|- Not verifying certified records meet litigation requirements
   104|- Failing to respond to mediator scheduling promptly (can delay case months)
   105|- Not updating the trial calendar when deadlines change
   106|
   107|## Related
   108|
   109|- [[premises-liability-case-patterns]] — PL cases are the most common path to litigation
   110|- [[statute-of-limitations-monitoring]] — SOL is the hard deadline for filing a complaint
   111|- [[attorney-review-triggers]] — all litigation decisions require attorney involvement
   112|- [[medical-records-collection]] — litigation requires certified records, not just copies
   113|", "total_lines": 112, "file_size": 15237, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|---
     2|title: Negotiation After Demand
     3|slug: negotiation-after-demand
     4|phase: [phase_4_negotiation]
     5|category: process
     6|tags: [negotiation, settlement, offer, counter, client-rejection, demand-followup]
     7|confidence: low
     8|evidence_count: 4
     9|created: 2026-04-12
    10|updated: 2026-04-12
    11|related:
    12|  - demand-preparation-workflow
    13|  - client-expectations-management
    14|  - attorney-review-triggers
    15|  - statute-of-limitations-monitoring
    16|---
    17|
    18|# Negotiation After Demand
    19|
    20|> After a demand is sent, the case enters negotiation with the insurance adjuster. This phase involves waiting for and evaluating the initial offer, communicating with the client about the offer, potential counter-offers, and either settlement or escalation to litigation. The negotiation phase can be complicated by client dissatisfaction, offer rejection, and lien resolution.
    21|
    22|## Pattern
    23|
    24|### Post-Demand Sequence
    25|1. **Demand Sent**: Follow-up task created for adjuster response
    26|2. **Adjuster Response**: Initial offer received (or adjuster requests more time/information)
    27|3. **Attorney Evaluation**: Attorney evaluates the offer against case value, medical specials, liens
    28|4. **Client Communication**: Client informed of offer and attorney recommendation
    29|5. **Decision Point**: Accept, counter-offer, or reject and prepare for litigation
    30|
    31|### Client Rejection Pattern
    32|A significant pattern emerges when clients reject settlement offers:
    33|- The case may be labeled "Settlement" prematurely (before client accepts)
    34|- Client calls in asking for an update, is told the case is "on settlement"
    35|- Client explicitly rejects the offer ("I am not settling")
    36|- The case must be re-classified from "Settlement" back to "Negotiation"
    37|- This back-and-forth creates frustration for all parties
    38|
    39|### Adjuster Follow-Up
    40|- Adjusters may need to be prompted for a response after demand
    41|- The firm sends follow-up communications to the adjuster
    42|- Lien amounts must be resolved during this phase to calculate the client's net recovery
    43|
    44|### Communication Challenges
    45|- Clients in negotiation become increasingly frustrated with the pace
    46|- Missed calls and voicemails escalate in urgency and frustration
    47|- Clients may threaten to "take another route" (change attorneys)
    48|- Regular, proactive communication is critical during this phase
    49|
    50|## Evidence
    51|
    52|- In an auto accident case, the demand was sent and the case moved to "Settlement" phase. However, the client explicitly refused the offer — a voicemail stated "I told you I am not settling for what you are... so come back." The phase had to be changed from "Settlement" back to "Negotiation." Staff confusion was evident: one team member noted "appears was settled but client didn't accept so maybe ongoing negotiations."
    53|- In the same case, the client's frustration escalated over months: text messages included "Hey are you going to get back with me or do I need to take another route. Let me know today" (6/2024) and multiple missed calls with voicemails demanding updates (3/2025, 8/2025, 10/2025, 2/2026, 3/2026). The attorney requested the adjuster pay a lien from Rawlings (10/2025), indicating lien resolution was happening during negotiation.
    54|- The case demonstrated a pattern where the firm told the client the case was "on settlement phase" and "we can't disclose the amount yet" — which created client frustration when the settlement did not materialize quickly.
    55|- In Case M (MVA, multi-plaintiff), the negotiation phase had a unique twist: the BI carrier tendered policy limits (25/50) within one day of the demand being sent, but settlement could not proceed because the limits had to be split among four injured plaintiffs represented by different firms. The negotiation shifted from firm-vs-insurer to inter-counsel allocation. The attorney emailed all co-plaintiffs' counsel to negotiate distribution. One attorney was slow to provide medical bills, stalling the process for months. The firm updated the client regularly but could only say "we are still waiting." When inter-counsel agreement was not reached, the case moved to litigation. This shows a novel negotiation pattern: the insurer has already paid limits, but the money cannot be distributed without all parties' agreement.
    56|
    57|## Agent Guidance
    58|
    59|1. After demand is sent, do NOT prematurely label the case as "Settlement" until the client has agreed to accept an offer
    60|2. When an offer is received, schedule a call with the client to discuss — do not communicate offers via text
    61|3. If the client rejects an offer, document the rejection clearly and move the case back to "Negotiation"
    62|4. Maintain regular contact during negotiation (at minimum every 2 weeks)
    63|5. When the client asks for updates, provide specific information about what is happening and what's next
    64|6. During negotiation, resolve liens in parallel — don't wait until after settlement to address them
    65|7. If the client threatens to leave, escalate to the attorney immediately for a personal call
    66|8. Track every client contact attempt (successful or missed) in the activity log
    67|
    68|## Pitfalls
    69|
    70|- Premature phase change to "Settlement" before client agreement
    71|- Telling the client the case is in "settlement" when it's actually in negotiation (creates false expectations)
    72|- Not communicating offer details to the client promptly
    73|- Allowing weeks/months of silence during active negotiation
    74|- Staff not knowing the current case status when client calls in
    75|- Not resolving liens during negotiation (delays final settlement)
    76|- Client losing trust due to poor communication (may terminate representation)
    77|
    78|## Related
    79|

    16|---
    17|
    18|# Health Insurance Lien Management
    19|
    20|> When a PI client has health insurance that pays accident-related medical bills, the health insurer asserts a subrogation lien — a right to be reimbursed from any settlement. Managing these liens involves verifying their accuracy, removing unrelated charges, and ultimately negotiating a reduction. Liens directly reduce the client's net recovery.
    21|
    22|## Pattern
    23|
    24|The health insurance lien lifecycle:
    25|
    26|1. **Identification**: The health insurer sends a "notice of subrogation claim" or "subrogation interest" letter once they learn of the accident
    27|2. **Itemization Request**: Request a detailed itemization of the lien showing each date of service and amount paid
    28|3. **Accuracy Audit**: Compare the lien itemization against the case's actual accident-related treatment
    29|4. **Dispute Unrelated Charges**: If the lien includes charges for treatment unrelated to the accident (pre-existing conditions, unrelated medical visits), request removal with supporting documentation
    30|5. **Updated Ledger**: Once disputed items are removed, obtain an updated lien ledger
    31|6. **Settlement Integration**: Factor the adjusted lien into the settlement math
    32|7. **Negotiation**: After settlement, negotiate a reduction of the lien (typically firms negotiate a percentage reduction)
    33|
    34|## Evidence
    35|
    36|- In Case C, the health insurer (Anthem) asserted a subrogation lien that included a 5/16/2024 date of service for an angiogram. The attorney identified that this procedure was scheduled prior to the accident (on 4/10/2024) for a pre-existing cardiac condition and was unrelated to the MVA. The paralegal emailed the lien handler (Carelon) requesting removal of the unrelated dates of service. An updated lien ledger with the charges removed was received the same day.
    37|- In Case D (chain-reaction MVA with 20+ providers), the case accumulated 6+ liens including health insurance (Optum at $1,707), provider liens (Cutting Edge Orthopedics at $25,763.75), and a hospital funding lien (Plano Surgical Hospital Funding LLC at $2,553.70). Multiple "Unknown" lien holders were also identified, illustrating that lien tracking becomes exponentially harder with more providers. Provider liens (as distinct from health insurance liens) may come from providers who treated on a lien basis.
    38|- In Case F (auto accident, dental injury), Anthem Blue Cross Blue Shield and Anthem Medicaid both asserted liens of $425.41 on the same case. The firm used ZipLiens to track the health insurance lien but initially sent the request to the wrong entity. Delta Dental of Kentucky also asserted a lien (amount unconfirmed). This demonstrates that dental injury cases can involve dental insurance liens in addition to health insurance liens — a distinct lien type not seen in standard MVA cases.
    39|- In Case G (auto accident, UIM litigation), Humana asserted a health insurance subrogation lien of $11,809. The case also involved pre-settlement funding ($31,200 from Legal Funding Partners), illustrating how the combination of health insurance liens plus funding can dramatically reduce client net recovery.
    40|- In Case H (auto accident, Medicaid), a Conduent lien of $20,669.08 was identified. Conduent is the third-party administrator for Kentucky Medicaid subrogation. The firm sent a first lien letter to Conduent at intake and later provided the Conduent lien information to the KAC adjuster during the claims process. Conduent called the firm to follow up on the case status, demonstrating that Medicaid subrogation administrators actively monitor cases.
    41|- In Case M (MVA, multi-plaintiff), multiple health insurance liens were identified early: Anthem BCBS, a managed care provider, and a pain management provider lien. The lien request was faxed to the health insurer on the day of file setup. Additionally, multiple "Unknown" lien holders appeared in the lien tracker, demonstrating that in cases with 6+ medical providers, identifying all subrogation interests takes extended effort. The case's policy limits (25/50 split among 4 plaintiffs) made lien resolution particularly critical since the per-plaintiff recovery was limited.
    42|- In Case N (premises liability estate, DOI 7/2020), a health insurance lien was identified from Humana at $5,887.97 (amount confirmed). Three additional "Unknown" lien holders were also listed. The Humana lien was identified early in the case via the case summary. The confirmed lien amount combined with the estate nature of the case (deceased client) means lien resolution will impact the surviving spouse's net recovery. This case further confirms the pattern that health insurance liens must be identified at intake and tracked throughout the case — even in litigation that spans 5+ years.
    43|
    44|## Agent Guidance
    45|
    46|1. When a subrogation notice arrives, log it immediately and request a detailed itemization
    47|2. Compare every date of service on the lien against the client's accident-related treatment dates
    48|3. Flag any dates of service that fall outside the treatment window or relate to unrelated conditions
    49|4. Draft a dispute letter to the lien handler citing the specific dates and reasons for removal
    50|5. Obtain pre-accident medical records if needed to prove a condition was pre-existing
    51|6. Keep the updated lien ledger in the case file for settlement calculations
    52|7. Alert the attorney whenever the lien amount changes materially
    53|
    54|## Pitfalls
    55|
    56|- Accepting the lien amount at face value without auditing it
    57|- Not knowing the client's pre-existing medical history (makes it impossible to identify unrelated charges)
    58|- Delaying the lien dispute — the longer you wait, the harder it is to resolve before settlement
    59|- Confusing the health insurance lien with PIP payments (they are separate obligations)
    60|- Not getting the updated lien ledger in writing after charges are removed
    61|
    62|### Government vs. Private Liens
    63|
    64|Note: Government entity liens (VA, Medicare, Medicaid) follow fundamentally different processes than private health insurance liens. See [[va-lien-management]] for the VA-specific lien process. Key differences include federal legal authority, longer timelines, and less flexibility in negotiation.
    65|
    66|## Related
    67|
    68|- [[pip-claims-management]] — PIP payments reduce what health insurance pays (and thus the lien)
    69|- [[demand-value-factors]] — the lien amount affects net recovery and settlement strategy
    70|- [[pre-existing-condition-separation]] — separating accident-related from pre-existing treatment
    71|- [[va-lien-management]] — VA liens are a distinct category requiring different handling
    72|", "total_lines": 71, "file_size": 6976, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

    16|---
    17|
    18|# Unreachable Client Patterns
    19|
    20|> Some PI clients are persistently difficult to reach — their voicemail is full, they don't return calls, and emails go unanswered. This pattern is distinct from client frustration (where the client is actively trying to reach the firm). Unreachable clients create a bottleneck because the firm needs their input to proceed with intake, treatment confirmation, and case management. Persistent multi-channel contact attempts are required, sometimes over weeks.
    21|
    22|## Pattern
    23|
    24|### Common Unreachability Signals
    25|1. **Voicemail full**: The client's voicemail box is full, preventing the firm from leaving a message
    26|2. **No answer, no callback**: Multiple outbound calls with no answer and no return call
    27|3. **Phone number changes**: Client changes phone numbers without notifying the firm
    28|4. **Email unresponsive**: Welcome emails and information requests go unanswered
    29|
    30|### Multi-Channel Escalation
    31|The firm follows a predictable escalation pattern when a client is unreachable:
    32|1. **Day 1**: Outbound call → no answer / voicemail full
    33|2. **Day 1**: Text message sent as fallback
    34|3. **Day 2-3**: Second outbound call attempt + email
    35|4. **Week 2**: Follow-up task created ("Two Week follow up with client")
    36|5. **Week 3-4**: Additional call attempts, emails, texts
    37|6. **Ongoing**: "Still unreachable" logged in case notes
    38|7. **Eventually**: Client responds via a different channel or new phone number
    39|
    40|### Impact on Case Timeline
    41|- Intake tasks cannot be completed without client input (providers list, insurance card, SSN)
    42|- File Setup stalls — the case sits in File Setup for months instead of progressing
    43|- Medical records requests are delayed because providers aren't confirmed
    44|- The case may ultimately be declined partly due to the difficulty of working it without client cooperation
    45|
    46|### Client Eventually Resurfaces
    47|Unreachable clients often do eventually make contact, sometimes with a new phone number. When they do:
    48|- They expect the case to be progressing despite their absence
    49|- They may provide the requested information belatedly
    50|- The firm must quickly re-engage and catch up on delayed tasks
    51|
    52|## Evidence
    53|
    54|- In an auto accident case (DOI 3/2024), the client was unreachable from day one of file setup. Multiple calls were made with the note "No answer, vm is full." Text messages were sent as fallback. The client eventually responded via text ("I will call you later, thanks") and then called back from a different number. The voicemail transcription showed they had also been trying to reach the firm: "We have missed each other for the last day or 2." Despite repeated outreach attempts over weeks, the client remained difficult to reach throughout the case. By 2/2025, the note still read "Still unreachable." The client's phone number changed at least once during the case.
    55|- In the same case, even when the firm was trying to close/decline the case, a staff member noted they needed to speak with the client about the case update — and had to use a different phone number than the one on file.
    56|- In a PL case (DOI 9/2023) in active litigation, the client called the attorney repeatedly (20+ missed calls over 7 months), but the calls went unreturned for extended periods. The attorney then made periodic outbound calls. This is a reverse unreachability pattern — the firm is unreachable to the client rather than vice versa, likely due to attorney bandwidth in a litigation-heavy caseload.
    57|- In an elderly MVA case (DOI 1/2022) in litigation, the client's voicemail was explicitly noted as "full" when the firm attempted to call with case updates. The client called back repeatedly but calls were not answered. This voicemail-full pattern prevents both parties from leaving messages, creating a complete communication breakdown.
    58|
    59|## Agent Guidance
    60|
    61|1. If the first call goes to voicemail and VM is full, immediately send a text message
    62|2. If no response to text, send an email the same day
    63|3. Set a 2-week follow-up task automatically on every case where the client has not responded
    64|4. Log EVERY contact attempt (call, text, email) with outcome (answered, VM full, no answer, etc.)
    65|5. After 3 failed contact attempts, escalate to the attorney with a note about unreachability
    66|6. Always ask the client for a secondary contact number and an emergency contact
    67|7. When the client does respond, immediately gather all needed information in that same conversation — you may not get another chance soon
    68|8. Record the client's updated phone number in the case system immediately when it changes
    69|9. Consider whether unreachability is a pattern that makes the case unworkable — flag for attorney review
    70|
    71|## Pitfalls
    72|
    73|- Only trying to call and not using text/email as fallback
    74|- Not logging failed contact attempts (creates the appearance of no effort)
    75|- Waiting weeks between contact attempts instead of escalating channels
    76|- Not asking for a secondary contact number at intake
    77|- Not updating the phone number in the system when the client calls from a new number
    78|- Assuming unreachability means the client is uninterested — they may be dealing with medical/life issues
    79|
    80|## Related
    81|
    82|- [[client-frustration-escalation]] — opposite pattern where the client is trying to reach the firm
    83|- [[client-expectations-management]] — unreachable clients may still have expectations about case progress
    84|- [[intake-and-onboarding-workflow]] — unreachability most critically impacts intake tasks
    85|- [[case-decline-and-closing-workflow]] — persistent unreachability may contribute to case decline decisions
    86|", "total_lines": 85, "file_size": 6042, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}
