    16|---
    17|
    18|# Medical Records and Bills Collection
    19|
    20|> Collecting complete medical records and bills from all treatment providers is a prerequisite for demand preparation. This process involves sending HIPAA authorizations, formal record/bill requests via fax and email, and persistent follow-up when providers are slow to respond. Gaps in records collection directly delay case resolution.
    21|
    22|## Pattern
    23|
    24|The records collection workflow follows this sequence:
    25|
    26|1. **Provider Identification**: Confirm all treatment providers with the client, including ER, PCP, specialists, imaging
    27|2. **Request Preparation**: Generate HIPAA medical authorization forms and formal records/billing request letters for each provider
    28|3. **Request Delivery**: Send via fax (using RingCentral or similar) and/or email to the provider's records department
    29|4. **Response Tracking**: Track which providers have responded with records vs. bills vs. both
    30|5. **Follow-Up**: If no response within 2-3 weeks, follow up
    31|6. **Client Assistance**: Ask clients to help obtain records from their patient portal (MyChart) when formal requests are delayed
    32|7. **Completeness Check**: Verify all providers are accounted for before moving to demand
    33|
    34|## Evidence
    35|
    36|- In Case B, records/billing requests were sent to St. Elizabeth Florence Hospital via email to a specific contact (Dawn.Probst@stelizabeth.com). The billing info was returned same day. However, getting complete records from Christ Hospital Physicians Primary Care took significantly longer.
    37|- In Case C, records were requested from Baptist Health Louisville via fax on 4/29/2024. Records were received by 5/13/2024 (14 days). A task to "review records and add to Timeline" was created once received.
    38|- In Case B, the client's spouse provided medical visit details from the patient portal (MyChart) while waiting for formal records, which helped move the case forward.
    39|- In Case C, the attorney flagged that records collection had stalled with "no action" for a month, emphasizing that negotiation cannot proceed without complete records.
    40|- In Case D (VA patient), records requests to VA Medical Center Lexington took over 12 months to yield results. Initial request mailed Nov 2023; VA OGC responded Jul 2024. Even after receiving records, they were incomplete — the client reported additional MRIs, physical therapy (VA IPRP program), and injections not in the file. The firm had to ask the client to review records and identify what was missing. The attorney noted: "We're missing a lot of stuff, and that's just part and parcel to the VA."
    41|- In Case D (premises liability, litigation), records had to be requested as CERTIFIED copies for use in litigation — standard copies were insufficient. The certification required correction (date range "04/2014 thru Current"). Records were delivered via Box.com file sharing from ROI quality analysts (e.g., Medicopy). Multiple providers were involved: Grace Health (PCP), Norton/Khalily (orthopedics), CHI St. Joseph (orthopedics + radiology), Baptist Health Corbin, and Corbin Imaging. Records were re-requested to get updated records during active litigation.
    42|- In Case E (auto accident), records review during demand preparation revealed referrals to providers not previously on file (White House Clinics, King's Eye Care). These had to be added and their records requested, delaying demand preparation. Records from one provider (H2 Health) were requested by calling, faxing, and re-faxing over weeks.
    43|- In Case D (20+ providers across 4 states), initial records/bills requests were sent in Feb-Apr 2023, but many providers had not responded by mid-2024, requiring a complete second round of "Certified Medical Records & Billing Request" letters in Aug 2024 — nearly 18 months after the first round. Multiple channels were used simultaneously (email, fax via RingCentral, ChartSwap, EpicLink). Provider confusion arose when billing names didn't match clinical names (e.g., "Norton Leatherman" vs "Norton Neuro & Spine" returned "No Records" despite bills existing).
    44|- In Case F (WC case), records were requested from Medical Center of Scottsville and Riverside Orthopedics via fax within the first week of file setup. The Med Center required a DocuSign-verified authorization (the standard fax copy was rejected), adding a round-trip delay. The provider also needed clarification on scope ("only ER records or all records"). Records arrived piecemeal — radiology CD first, then billing statements separately. A third-party billing entity (TeamHealth) sent bills that were associated with the hospital visit but under a different entity name. Multiple new providers were added over 2+ years as treatment progressed (pain management, shoulder injections, MRI facilities).
    45|- In Case H (MVA, air evac), records collection involved an air ambulance service (Air Evac EMS) requiring fax to a distant location. ChartSwap was used for TriStar Skyline Medical Center records retrieval. The case involved records from providers across multiple states (TN hospital where air-lifted, KY local providers). The air evacuation bill alone was $72K+, making complete billing documentation critical.
    46|- In Case G (MVA, companion cases), records collection was complicated by a companion case (daughter) requiring separate hospital records from Children's Hospital. The client was asked to obtain companion records but failed to do so over months (Oct 2024 to Feb 2025+). Chiropractic records from Southgate Chiropractic were also needed.
    47|- In Case I (MVA), records were requested from UofL Health St. Mary & Elizabeth Hospital and chiropractic provider via fax within the first week. Provider sent records via ChartSwap platform. The PIP adjuster proactively asked about medical records even before the firm's formal requests were complete.
    48|- In a PL case (retail slip-and-fall, 10+ providers), records collection extended over 2+ years. Even during active litigation (July 2025), the firm was still making outbound calls to providers (orthopedics, therapy, PCP) for outstanding records and bills. The medical authorization form was rejected by one provider because it was missing the date of the patient's signature and their DOB — requiring client callback and resubmission, confirming that incomplete authorization forms are a recurring records collection bottleneck. ChartSwap was used as an intermediary for some provider records.
    49|- In an MVA case with companion plaintiffs, certified medical records were requested from a university hospital and chiropractic provider for litigation. A billing error was discovered in the chiropractic records (incorrect certified billing amount) requiring correction correspondence. This confirms that certified records must be audited — providers can certify incorrect billing amounts. Bills from EMS providers were also collected for PIP submission. The case used EvenUP for case valuation, requiring complete records before the evaluation could be generated.
    50|- In Case M (MVA, multi-plaintiff, DOI 8/2023), records were requested from 6 providers including EMS, a university hospital system (separate hospital and physician entities), chiropractic, pharmacy, and pain management. The hospital system had distinct billing entities — hospital bills were requested separately from physician bills, and each was received on different dates (spanning Nov-Dec 2024). The PIP claim was set up same day and PIP info provided to the hospital immediately. The firm also requested and received the police report from the out-of-state police department (New Albany, IN) — the report was not immediately available the day after the accident and required follow-up.
    51|- In Case N (premises liability estate, DOI 7/2020), records collection was complicated by provider misidentification. The firm initially requested records from "CareTenders" (a home health agency), only to discover no records existed — the actual provider was a different home health company. The surviving spouse later provided a calendar from the correct provider, enabling the correct request. Records from one diagnostic imaging provider were requested via ChartSwap, while records from the hospital required a separate LOR to the legal department before records could be released. Bills for the home health provider remained outstanding even 3+ years later during active litigation — the attorney noted during deposition prep that bills were missing. This confirms that provider misidentification is a persistent risk, especially for home health and similar ancillary providers where patients may not know the exact entity name.
    52|
    53|## Agent Guidance
    54|
    55|1. Create a provider checklist during file setup with records requested/received dates for each
    56|2. Send HIPAA authorizations and record requests within the first week of file setup
    57|3. Use both fax and email when possible — some providers respond faster to one channel
    58|4. Set follow-up tasks at 14-day intervals for each outstanding request
    59|5. Encourage clients to share records from patient portals as an interim measure
    60|6. Track records and bills separately — receiving one does not mean you have the other
    61|7. Do not move to demand preparation until ALL providers have been accounted for
    62|8. When the attorney asks about case status, have the records collection status ready
    63|
    64|## Pitfalls
    65|
    66|- Not confirming all providers with the client early (discovering a provider late delays everything)
    67|- Sending requests and not tracking responses
    68|- Not distinguishing between records received and bills received (you need both)
    69|- Letting the client's medical portal records substitute for formal provider records in the demand
    70|- Waiting too long to follow up on non-responsive providers
    71|- Requesting records under a billing entity name that differs from the clinical entity name (causes "no records found" responses)
    72|- Not anticipating that multi-state providers require different request methods and response timelines
    73|
    74|## Related
    75|
    76|- [[pip-claims-management]] — PIP needs bills to process; records collection feeds PIP workflow
    77|- [[demand-timing-strategy]] — demand cannot be sent until records are complete
    78|- [[provider-communication-patterns]] — how different providers respond to requests
    79|- [[government-entity-records-collection]] — VA and other government providers require distinct handling with much longer timelines
    80|- [[premises-liability-case-patterns]] — PL cases in litigation require certified records
    81|- [[demand-preparation-workflow]] — records completion gates the demand phase
    82|", "total_lines": 81, "file_size": 10934, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

    15|---
    16|
    17|# Adjuster Reassignment Patterns
    18|
    19|> Insurance carriers frequently reassign adjusters during the life of a PI claim, sometimes multiple times. Each reassignment resets the negotiation relationship and often causes delays as the new adjuster reviews the file from scratch. In long-running cases, the firm may interact with 3-5 different adjusters on a single claim. Understanding this pattern helps manage expectations and maintain negotiation momentum.
    20|
    21|## Pattern
    22|
    23|Adjuster reassignment follows observable patterns:
    24|
    25|1. **Initial Adjuster**: Handles the claim from opening through liability investigation. May make the first offer.
    26|2. **Mid-Case Reassignment**: Common at 6-12 months. May coincide with carrier restructuring or adjuster caseload rebalancing.
    27|3. **Stale File Reassignment**: When a case has had no movement for extended periods, carriers may reassign to a "closer" adjuster who proactively reaches out to resolve.
    28|4. **New Adjuster Introduction**: New adjusters typically introduce themselves, note they've been "reassigned" to the file, and request updated documentation.
    29|5. **Fresh Eyes Effect**: A new adjuster sometimes brings movement — they re-evaluate the file without the baggage of prior negotiations and may make a new offer that differs from the prior adjuster's position.
    30|
    31|### Impact on Negotiations
    32|
    33|- Each reassignment adds 2-4 weeks of delay as the new adjuster reviews the file
    34|- New adjusters may not honor informal commitments made by prior adjusters
    35|- A new adjuster may request documentation already provided to the prior adjuster
    36|- However, reassignment can be an opportunity: the new adjuster may be more willing to negotiate than a predecessor who was entrenched in a position
    37|
    38|## Evidence
    39|
    40|- In Case D, the BI claim went through at least 3 adjusters over 2+ years:
    41|  - Adjuster 1 (initial): Handled the claim during the liability investigation phase, while coverage was still "under review"
    42|  - Adjuster 2: Made the initial offer. After receiving updated records, the claim was "reassigned to a different adjuster" with the hope it would "cause some movement."
    43|  - Adjuster 3: Self-identified as "the new adjuster on this file." Left multiple voicemails proactively reaching out, noting "there really has not been any movement" and "I was hoping to... try to follow up." This adjuster made multiple attempts to reach the attorney over several weeks before connecting.
    44|  - Adjuster 4: Subsequently reassigned again; the new adjuster reviewed additional treatment records and made the carrier's "top offer."
    45|- The Adjuster 3 reassignment was particularly notable: this adjuster was proactive, called multiple times, left detailed voicemails expressing willingness to negotiate, and explicitly said "I think that if we could have a phone conversation about this, we could get it resolved."
    46|- After submitting updated records, the carrier reassigned to Adjuster 4, who raised the offer but also introduced a new defense (attributing treatment to an unrelated slip-and-fall incident).
    47|
    48|## Agent Guidance
    49|
    50|1. When a new adjuster introduces themselves, log their name, direct phone, extension, and email immediately.
    51|2. Proactively send the new adjuster a summary of the case status and any prior correspondence/offers.
    52|3. Treat a new adjuster as an opportunity — respond promptly to their outreach.
    53|4. If a new adjuster calls and leaves a voicemail, return the call within 24 hours (they are motivated to work the file).
    54|5. Keep a chronological log of all adjusters who have handled the claim.
    55|6. When the new adjuster requests documentation, send it promptly rather than assuming they have the prior adjuster's files.
    56|7. Alert the attorney when an adjuster change occurs, as this may be a strategic opportunity to re-engage.
    57|
    58|## Pitfalls
    59|
    60|- Ignoring a new adjuster's outreach (especially when they are proactively trying to settle)
    61|- Assuming the new adjuster has all documentation from the prior adjuster
    62|- Not updating contact information when a reassignment occurs
    63|- Missing the window of opportunity when a "closer" adjuster is assigned and motivated
    64|- Letting weeks pass without responding to a new adjuster's repeated outreach
    65|
    66|## Related
    67|
    68|- [[treatment-gap-defense]] — new adjusters may re-evaluate treatment gap arguments
    69|- [[client-expectations-management]] — adjuster changes add to client frustration about delays
    70|- [[statute-of-limitations-monitoring]] — adjusters may reference the approaching SOL as urgency to settle
    71|", "total_lines": 70, "file_size": 4869, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

    14|---
    15|
    16|# Client Communication Through Family Members
    17|
    18|> In many PI cases, the primary point of contact is a family member (spouse, parent) rather than the injured client directly. This pattern creates both efficiencies (an engaged advocate) and risks (communication gaps, authorization requirements). Managing these relationships requires clear protocols around authorization, documentation, and expectation-setting.
    19|
    20|## Pattern
    21|
    22|Two common scenarios emerge:
    23|
    24|**Spouse as Primary Contact**: The spouse handles all communication, forwards medical documents, asks status questions, and relays information to the injured client. The injured client may have signed the retainer but is less directly involved in day-to-day case management.
    25|
    26|**Parent with POA**: When a client is incarcerated or otherwise unavailable, a parent with Power of Attorney may serve as the authorized representative. The POA document must be obtained and verified before the firm can discuss case details.
    27|
    28|In both scenarios, the family member tends to:
    29|- Be more proactive about asking for updates
    30|- Forward bills and medical documents as they arrive
    31|- Express urgency about resolution ("we want this to go away")
    32|- Ask about the impact of unpaid bills on credit
    33|
    34|## Evidence
    35|
    36|- In Case B, the client's husband was the primary communicator throughout the entire case. He forwarded all medical bills, relayed appointment results, asked about case status repeatedly, and expressed concern about bills going to collections. The client herself was largely absent from communications.
    37|- In Case A, the client was incarcerated and the client's mother (with POA) was the sole point of contact. The mother provided intake information, the POA document, and followed up on case status.
    38|- In Case H (MVA, air evac), the client designated a go-between ("Anna"/Ann Dattillo) as the intermediary for all communications. The firm was instructed to communicate through Anna "per Client instructions." Anna was added as an associated contact. She requested copies of the deposition and case updates. When the attorney changed (Bryce Koon departed), the new attorney had to re-establish the communication chain through Anna. The client herself called separately, creating confusion about who was the primary contact.
    39|- In Case G (MVA, companion cases), the client was the father managing his own case AND his daughter's companion case. He needed to coordinate daughter's medical records, hospital visits, and communicate on her behalf. Multiple calls over months tried to get the companion's hospital records — the father was asked to speak to the daughter and obtain them, but this remained incomplete for 6+ months.
    40|- In Case H (MVA), initial intake was through a witness/companion ("Robert Grant") who called in. He was given the office number and told the client's case manager name. He provided pictures and information on behalf of the client. This shows that in some cases, even the initial referral comes through a third party.
    41|- In Case N (premises liability estate, DOI 7/2020), the injured client died and the surviving spouse became the sole point of contact for the estate case. The spouse handled all communications, called for updates repeatedly, completed probate inventory, and relayed information from the home health provider that corrected a records request error (wrong provider name). The spouse showed classic frustration escalation — calling for updates, being told the attorney would call back, then calling again when no callback came. This is a distinct variant of the family-as-contact pattern: when the client has died, the family member is not just a proxy but the actual party (as estate representative), adding both legal and emotional complexity.
    42|
    43|## Agent Guidance
    44|
    45|1. During intake, identify who the primary point of contact will be
    46|2. If it's a family member, verify authorization: POA for non-spouse, general authorization for spouse
    47|3. Document the authorized contact in the case file with their contact info
    48|4. Set expectations with the family member about case timeline and the process
    49|5. When the family member forwards bills/records, acknowledge receipt promptly and explain next steps
    50|6. When the family member expresses urgency, respond with specific status updates rather than vague reassurances
    51|7. If bills are going to collections, explain the process (PIP submission, timeline) proactively
    52|
    53|## Pitfalls
    54|
    55|- Discussing case details with a family member who has not been properly authorized
    56|- Assuming the family member is accurately relaying information to/from the client
    57|- Not setting expectations with the family member about realistic case timelines
    58|- Ignoring repeated follow-up inquiries from family members (this erodes trust)
    59|- Not having a clear protocol for who can authorize settlement decisions
    60|
    61|## Related
    62|
    63|- [[client-expectations-management]] — managing expectations is crucial when family members push for faster resolution
    64|- [[intake-via-referral]] — cases often come through family referrals
    65|", "total_lines": 64, "file_size": 5434, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

     7|confidence: high

     9|created: 2026-04-12

    12|  - medical-records-collection

    14|  - attorney-review-triggers
    15|---
    16|
    17|# Case Phase Transitions
    18|
    19|> PI cases follow a defined lifecycle: Lead -> Potential New Client -> File Setup -> Treatment -> Demand in Progress -> Negotiation -> Settlement. Each transition has specific prerequisites and is triggered by specific events. Understanding these transitions is critical for case management automation.
    20|
    21|## Pattern
    22|
    23|Observed phase transitions across cases:
    24|
    25|### Lead -> File Setup (or via Potential New Client)
    26|- **Trigger**: Intake docs signed, client info received
    27|- **Prerequisites**: Signed fee agreement, HIPAA authorization, basic client info
    28|- **Actions on transition**: Create welcome call task, send welcome email, set up insurance claims, begin records requests
    29|- **Timeline**: Same day to a few days after initial contact
    30|
    31|### File Setup -> Treatment
    32|- **Trigger**: All providers confirmed, initial records/bills requests sent
    33|- **Prerequisites**: Insurance claims set up, LOR sent, medical providers identified
    34|- **Actions on transition**: Begin treatment monitoring, send records requests
    35|- **Timeline**: Days after file setup
    36|
    37|### Treatment -> Demand in Progress
    38|- **Trigger**: Client confirms treatment has concluded, or attorney directs
    39|- **Prerequisites**: Treatment complete, preferably all records received
    40|- **Actions on transition**: Confirm treatment status with client, request outstanding records/bills, begin demand preparation
    41|- **Timeline**: Weeks to months after treatment ends (waiting for records)
    42|
    43|### Demand in Progress -> Negotiation
    44|- **Trigger**: Demand package sent to adjuster
    45|- **Prerequisites**: All records/bills received, demand drafted and approved
    46|- **Note**: In transfer cases, may skip directly to Negotiation
    47|
    48|### Negotiation -> Litigation
    49|- **Trigger**: Inadequate offer, SOL approaching, or adjuster intransigence
    50|- **Prerequisites**: Attorney decision to file, complaint drafted
    51|- **Actions on transition**: File complaint, serve defendants, begin discovery; negotiation may continue in parallel
    52|- **Timeline**: Observed at approximately 1 year post-accident when initial offer was very low
    53|
    54|## Evidence
    55|
    56|- In Case A, the case went directly from Lead to File Setup to Negotiation (skipping Treatment and Demand) because the prior firm had already handled treatment and sent a demand.
    57|- In Case B, the transition from Treatment to Demand in Progress was triggered by the attorney noting "no activity since August" and directing staff to check in with the client. Once treatment was confirmed complete, the phase change occurred.
    58|- In Case C, the case went through Lead -> File Setup -> Treatment sequentially, with clear phase change entries in the activity log.
    59|- In Case D, the case progressed through File Setup (July 2023) -> Treatment (July 2023) -> Negotiation. However, the negotiation phase lasted over 2 years due to VA records delays, multiple adjuster reassignments, and incomplete medical documentation. The attorney eventually ordered "prepare and send the demand" in September 2025 (27 months after the accident) despite incomplete records. This illustrates that VA cases can dramatically extend the typical case timeline, and that at some point the attorney must make a judgment call to proceed with available information rather than waiting for perfection.
    60|- In Case D (auto accident, DOI 2/2023), the case followed the full lifecycle: File Setup -> Treatment -> Demand in Progress -> Settlement -> back to Negotiation. The backwards transition from Settlement to Negotiation occurred because the client refused the settlement offer. This shows that phase transitions are not always forward-only.
    61|- In Case E (premises liability, DOI 4/2019), the case skipped the Demand phase entirely and went into litigation (complaint filed, discovery, mediation). PL cases may bypass the standard demand-negotiation flow and proceed directly to suit.
    62|- In Case D (chain-reaction MVA, multiple defendants), the case transitioned from Negotiation to Litigation when the BI adjuster's first offer (~$13K for a concussion/spine case with $60K+ in treatment) was deemed inadequate. A complaint was filed in Jefferson Circuit Court approximately 1 year post-accident. Litigation activities (discovery, depositions, IMEs, mediation summaries) ran in parallel with continued negotiation against a second carrier (Progressive). The case remained in the "negotiation" status in Filevine despite active litigation, revealing that the system status and actual workflow can diverge.
    63|- In Case F (auto accident, dental injury), the case followed: PNC -> File Setup -> Treatment -> Litigation. Treatment was prolonged because the client needed a tooth replacement (dental implant) which extended treatment by over a year. The case moved to litigation without going through a formal demand phase, with a complaint filed approximately 22 months post-accident. This illustrates that dental injury cases can bypass the standard demand phase when treatment extends near the SOL.
    64|- In Case G (auto accident, UIM), the case followed: PNC -> File Setup -> Treatment -> Litigation. A BI claim and a UIM claim ran on parallel tracks. The UIM complaint was filed in a different court (Fayette Circuit) than the BI complaint would have been filed. The case demonstrates that UIM claims require a separate litigation track when the at-fault driver's policy limits are insufficient.
    65|- In Case H (auto accident, stolen vehicle), the case followed: PNC -> Pre-Negotiation -> Demand in Progress -> Litigation. The transition to litigation was forced by the BI carrier's coverage denial (vehicle was reported stolen). The complaint was filed approximately 13 months post-accident. The case then remained in litigation for 3+ years, with discovery, depositions, and multiple defense counsel involved. This is the earliest observed litigation filing, driven by coverage denial rather than inadequate offers.
    66|- In Case G (MVA, companion cases), the case progressed through File Setup -> Treatment -> Demand -> Litigation in approximately 1 year. Complaint filed in Boone County (24-CI-00452). The case went through full litigation including discovery, depositions, and mediation (via Zoom). Mediation failed with last-best-final offers deemed inadequate. This confirms the pattern that filing suit and going through litigation is a common path when pre-suit resolution fails.
    67|- In Case H (MVA, air evac), the case progressed through File Setup -> Treatment -> Demand in Progress -> Litigation. Complaint filed in Barren County (23-CI-00659). The case involved a parallel subrogation action (KFBMIC v. client in District Court). Discovery, depositions, and mediation scheduling all followed. The case remained in "treatment" status in Filevine despite active litigation — another example of system status diverging from actual workflow.
    68|- In Case I (MVA, disputed liability), the case moved from File Setup -> Treatment in a single day. After BI carrier accepted only 25% liability, the firm filed a complaint in Jefferson Circuit Court (~8 months post-accident). The disputed liability was the primary litigation trigger rather than low offers or SOL pressure.
    69|- In Case F (WC case), the phase structure differed from PI: the case involved DWC (Department of Workers' Compensation) proceedings including BRC (Benefit Review Conferences) rather than standard PI phases. This case type uses WC-specific milestones (DWC forms, BRC hearings, IME scheduling through WC process) that map differently to the standard PHASE_DAG.
    70|- In a workers' compensation case (DOI 1/2024), the phase went directly from Potential New Client to Treatment (skipping File Setup), reflecting that WC cases have a different phase structure. The WC carrier accepted the claim and the client was directed to treatment. Deposition scheduling with defense counsel occurred while the case was still in "treatment" phase — confirming that WC cases don't follow the same phase sequence as auto accident PI cases.
    71|- In a litigation case (DOI 1/2021), the case spent 4+ years in litigation: complaint filed in 2021, discovery completed in 2022, depositions scheduled in 2023, motions filed in 2023-2024, and police records collection continuing through late 2024. The case was still in active litigation as of late 2025 with defense counsel communications ongoing. This is the longest observed case timeline, illustrating that complex multi-defendant litigation can extend far beyond the typical 2-3 year PI case lifecycle.
    72|- In an auto accident case (DOI 3/2024), the case was opened in File Setup but was moved directly to "Closing" after investigation revealed no BI or UM coverage (PIP-only through KAC). The phase transition File Setup -> Closing bypasses all intermediate phases. This confirms that case decline is a terminal phase transition that can occur from File Setup without passing through Treatment, Demand, or Negotiation.
    73|- In Case M (MVA, DOI 8/2023), the case progressed rapidly through File Setup -> Treatment (2 days) -> Demand in Progress (5 months) -> Negotiation (same day demand sent, 1/10/2024). A third-party demand tool (EvenUP) was used to prepare the demand, which was saved to the file then edited and sent to both BI carriers. The Demand in Progress -> Negotiation transition was essentially instantaneous once the demand was sent. However, the Negotiation phase stalled for months because the BI policy limits needed to be split among four co-plaintiffs represented by different firms. When inter-counsel distribution negotiations failed, the case transitioned to Litigation (complaint filed in Floyd County). This is a novel litigation trigger: not low offers, not coverage denial, not SOL pressure — but rather failure to agree on policy limits allocation among co-plaintiffs.
    74|- In Case N (premises liability estate, DOI 7/2020), the case went from Pre-Negotiation directly to Demand in Progress on 10/20/2020, just 6 days after the accident report was obtained. The case then moved to litigation (complaint filed in Jefferson Circuit Court 21-CI-003798) and has remained in litigation for 5+ years. This confirms the pattern that premises liability cases frequently bypass extended negotiation and proceed to litigation. The case also involved a referral to outside counsel (Joel Franklin) during the litigation phase, indicating that PL cases against institutional defendants may require specialist litigation counsel.
    75|
    76|## Agent Guidance
    77|
    78|1. Track phase change prerequisites as a checklist
    79|2. Do not advance a phase until all prerequisites are met
    80|3. Log every phase change with the person who authorized it and the date
    81|4. When an attorney asks about a case, report the current phase and what's blocking the next transition
    82|5. For transfer cases, evaluate which phases were already completed by the prior firm
    83|6. The attorney should approve all phase transitions from Demand in Progress onward
    84|
    85|## Pitfalls
    86|
    87|- Advancing to Negotiation without a demand in the file (observed in Case A)
    88|- Not confirming treatment completion with the client before transitioning to Demand
    89|- Letting cases sit in Treatment for months without checking on client status
    90|- Not logging the reason for the phase change
    91|- Prematurely changing to "Settlement" before client has agreed (observed in Case D — had to reverse to Negotiation)
    92|- Staff confusion about current phase when client calls for an update
    93|
    94|## Related
    95|
    96|- [[medical-records-collection]] — records completion is a prerequisite for Demand
    97|- [[demand-timing-strategy]] — timing of the phase transition to Demand matters
    98|- [[attorney-review-triggers]] — certain transitions require attorney review
    99|- [[premises-liability-case-patterns]] — PL cases have a different phase pattern (often skipping demand)
   100|- [[demand-preparation-workflow]] — demand phase entry requires specific prerequisites
   101|", "total_lines": 100, "file_size": 12256, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

    16|---
    17|
    18|# Underinsured Motorist (UIM) Claim Management
    19|
    20|> When the at-fault driver's BI policy limits are insufficient to cover the plaintiff's damages, the plaintiff may pursue an Underinsured Motorist (UIM) claim against their own auto insurance carrier. UIM claims run as a parallel track to the BI claim and require distinct procedural steps including consent-to-settle notifications, separate complaint filing, and independent adjuster management — often with the same carrier that provides the client's PIP coverage.
    21|
    22|## Pattern
    23|
    24|UIM claim management follows a parallel-track structure alongside the BI claim:
    25|
    26|### Setup
    27|1. **Identify UIM Coverage**: During file setup, check whether the client's own auto policy includes UIM coverage. If the at-fault driver's BI limits are low relative to the client's injuries, UIM becomes relevant.
    28|2. **Separate Claim Setup**: The UIM claim requires its own claim number and adjuster, even when the UIM carrier is the same company providing PIP.
    29|3. **Consent to Settle**: Before accepting the BI settlement, the UIM carrier must be notified and given an opportunity to consent (or subrogate). This is a critical procedural requirement.
    30|
    31|### Parallel Management
    32|- The UIM adjuster is separate from the PIP adjuster at the same carrier
    33|- The UIM claim number may be the same as or different from the PIP claim number
    34|- The UIM complaint is filed in the appropriate court (may be different jurisdiction than BI)
    35|- UIM litigation follows standard discovery and deposition procedures
    36|- The UIM carrier files an answer to the complaint and may retain defense counsel
    37|
    38|### Litigation
    39|- UIM complaints are filed against the client's own insurer — an adversarial posture against the client's own carrier
    40|- A courtesy copy of the complaint should be sent to the UIM adjuster
    41|- Discovery includes the plaintiff's first set of interrogatories/production requests to the UIM carrier
    42|- UIM litigation can proceed independently from BI settlement
    43|
    44|## Evidence
    45|
    46|- In Case G (auto accident, DOI 7/30/2023), the client's extensive injuries (including $45,830+ hospital bills, physical therapy, chiropractic care, and surgery) exceeded the at-fault driver's BI policy limits (State Farm, claim #1754K417G). The client had UIM coverage through Allstate Insurance (claim #723447272). The UIM claim required separate management:
    47|  - PIP adjuster (Margaret Sharp) handled PIP claim through Allstate
    48|  - UIM adjuster (Nick Burchette) handled the UIM claim separately
    49|  - A UIM complaint was filed in Fayette Circuit Court (24-CI-03580, "McCay v. Allstate Property and Casualty Insurance")
    50|  - Allstate filed an answer to the complaint
    51|  - A consent-to-settle form was obtained from Allstate before the BI claim could proceed
    52|  - Discovery commenced with plaintiff's first set of discovery to Allstate
    53|  - The case involved $319.82 in court filing fees, plus expenses for EvenUP ($500) and Digital Owl ($495) for case evaluation
    54|  - The client also had a Humana health insurance lien ($11,809) and pre-settlement funding ($31,200 from Legal Funding Partners)
    55|  - Defense counsel (Jennifer Ann Peterson) was retained by Allstate
    56|  - A separate "waiving subrogation" document was filed
    57|
    58|## Agent Guidance
    59|
    60|1. At intake, identify the client's own auto insurance UIM coverage limits
    61|2. Compare the at-fault driver's BI limits to estimated damages — if damages likely exceed BI limits, flag for UIM
    62|3. Set up the UIM claim as a separate insurance claim record, even if it's the same carrier as PIP
    63|4. Track the UIM adjuster separately from the PIP adjuster
    64|5. Before accepting any BI settlement offer, obtain consent-to-settle from the UIM carrier
    65|6. Send a courtesy copy of the UIM complaint to the UIM adjuster when filed
    66|7. Track UIM litigation deadlines independently from BI claim deadlines
    67|8. When evaluating the total case value, consider BI limits + UIM limits as the recovery ceiling
    68|9. Budget for additional expenses: UIM litigation may require case evaluation tools (EvenUP, Digital Owl) and additional records requests
    69|
    70|## Pitfalls
    71|
    72|- Forgetting to check for UIM coverage during intake
    73|- Not obtaining consent-to-settle from the UIM carrier before accepting BI settlement (can waive UIM rights)
    74|- Confusing the PIP adjuster with the UIM adjuster at the same carrier
    75|- Not filing the UIM complaint timely (UIM has its own SOL considerations)
    76|- Not recognizing that UIM litigation is adversarial against the client's own carrier
    77|- Failing to track UIM litigation expenses separately
    78|
    79|## Related
    80|
    81|- [[pip-claims-management]] — PIP and UIM are often at the same carrier but managed separately
    82|- [[multi-carrier-negotiation]] — UIM adds another parallel claim track
    83|- [[litigation-process-patterns]] — UIM litigation follows standard litigation procedures
    84|- [[statute-of-limitations-monitoring]] — UIM claims have their own filing deadlines
    85|", "total_lines": 84, "file_size": 5345, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

     9|created: 2026-04-12

    16|---
    17|
    18|# Multi-Carrier Claim Coordination
    19|
    20|> Many PI cases involve simultaneous claims with multiple insurance carriers and government entities: a BI (Bodily Injury) claim against the at-fault party's insurer, a PIP claim for medical payments, and one or more liens (health insurance, VA, Medicare/Medicaid). Coordinating these parallel claims — each with different adjusters, timelines, and processes — is one of the most complex operational challenges in PI practice. Failure to coordinate can result in double-billing disputes, delayed settlements, and reduced client recovery.
    21|
    22|## Pattern
    23|
    24|A complex multi-carrier case involves these parallel streams:
    25|
    26|### Stream 1: BI (Bodily Injury) Claim
    27|- Against the at-fault party's insurer
    28|- Dependent on completed records for demand preparation
    29|- Negotiation involves offers, counter-offers, and the final settlement
    30|- The BI settlement is the primary source of client recovery
    31|
    32|### Stream 2: PIP (Personal Injury Protection) Claim
    33|- Through the client's own insurer (in PIP states like KY)
    34|- Bills submitted with "direction to pay" as they come in
    35|- PIP can pay providers directly, including paying VA liens
    36|- PIP payments reduce the amount that must come from the BI settlement
    37|- PIP ledger needed before BI demand is sent
    38|
    39|### Stream 3: Government/Health Insurance Liens
    40|- VA liens, Medicare conditional payments, health insurance subrogation
    41|- Must be identified, quantified, and factored into settlement math
    42|- Payment may come from PIP (before settlement) or from settlement proceeds
    43|- Federal liens (VA, Medicare) have different legal frameworks than private liens
    44|
    45|### Coordination Points
    46|
    47|The critical coordination moments are:
    48|
    49|1. **File Setup**: Identify ALL insurance streams and liens. Set up separate tracking for each.
    50|2. **Bill Routing**: Each medical bill must be routed to the correct payer:
    51|   - VA treatment bills → VA lien (may be paid by PIP)
    52|   - Private provider bills → PIP first, then any remainder to health insurance
    53|   - Bills not covered by PIP/health insurance → BI demand specials
    54|3. **PIP Directing to Pay VA Lien**: PIP carriers can be directed to pay VA liens directly, effectively using the client's PIP benefits to satisfy the government lien. This reduces the VA lien's impact on the BI settlement.
    55|4. **Demand Preparation**: Before sending the BI demand, you need:
    56|   - Updated PIP ledger showing all payments
    57|   - Current VA lien amount (adjusted for any PIP payments)
    58|   - All medical specials from all providers
    59|5. **Settlement Math**: The BI settlement must account for remaining unpaid liens, attorney fees, and case costs. Multi-carrier cases have more deductions, often leaving less for the client.
    60|
    61|### The "New Money" vs. "Total Value" Distinction
    62|
    63|In multi-carrier cases, the BI adjuster may frame offers as "new money" — meaning money on top of what PIP and other sources have already paid. This distinction is important:
    64|- Total medical specials: Sum of all treatment costs
    65|- Already paid by PIP: Reduces what the BI settlement needs to cover
    66|- VA lien amount: Must be satisfied (may already be paid by PIP)
    67|- "New money" from BI: What the BI carrier pays in addition to PIP payments
    68|- Client's net: New money minus attorney fees, costs, and any remaining liens
    69|
    70|## Evidence
    71|
    72|- In Case D, the case involved three carriers and a government lien simultaneously:
    73|  - BI claim with a small regional carrier (at-fault party's insurer)
    74|  - PIP claim with a national carrier (client's own insurer)
    75|  - VA lien managed by VA OGC Revenue Law Group ($4,437.13)
    76|  - The PIP carrier was directed to pay the VA lien directly, which it did
    77|  - The BI adjuster framed his offer as "new money" of $4,200 on top of the VA payment, saying "the VA wants their money back" and noting the PIP had already handled the VA balance
    78|  - Later adjusters increased the offer, but also introduced defenses (attributing treatment to an unrelated incident)
    79|  - The final settlement math required tracking: PIP payments, VA lien satisfaction, chiro bills (disputed for relatedness), PT bills, and the BI offer
    80|- The PIP adjuster disputed the relatedness of chiropractic treatment, noting records indicated the treatment was for a condition that "gotten worse a month prior for no apparent reason" and requesting "additional documentation indicating relatedness."
    81|
    82|## Agent Guidance
    83|
    84|1. At file setup, create a coordination matrix listing every carrier, claim number, adjuster, and lien:
    85|   | Stream | Carrier | Claim # | Adjuster | Status |
    86|   |--------|---------|---------|----------|--------|
    87|   | BI | [Carrier] | [#] | [Name] | [Phase] |
    88|   | PIP | [Carrier] | [#] | [Name] | [Phase] |
    89|   | VA Lien | VA OGC | [GCL#] | [Name] | [Phase] |
    90|2. Track bill routing: for each medical bill, record which carrier(s) it was submitted to and the payment status.
    91|3. When a VA lien is identified and PIP is available, discuss with the attorney whether to direct PIP to pay the VA lien.
    92|4. Before preparing the BI demand, ensure you have current ledgers/amounts from ALL streams.
    93|5. When presenting settlement offers to the attorney, show the complete multi-carrier picture:
    94|   - BI offer (new money)
    95|   - PIP payments already made
    96|   - VA/lien status (satisfied by PIP or outstanding)
    97|   - Net to client after all deductions
    98|6. When a PIP adjuster disputes relatedness of treatment, obtain additional documentation from the provider and resubmit.
    99|7. Monitor SOL separately — the BI statute may expire while you are still coordinating VA records/liens.
   100|
   101|## Pitfalls
   102|
   103|- Not identifying all insurance streams at intake
   104|- Losing track of which bills have been submitted to which carrier
   105|- Not understanding the "new money" distinction when evaluating BI offers
   106|- Letting VA lien delays hold up the entire BI negotiation
   107|- Not using PIP to pay VA liens when PIP benefits are available
   108|- Assuming the PIP adjuster will accept all bills as accident-related (they audit for relatedness too)
   109|- Not having complete ledgers from all carriers before preparing the BI demand
   110|
   111|## Related
   112|
   113|- [[pip-claims-management]] — PIP claim management as one stream in multi-carrier coordination
   114|- [[va-lien-management]] — VA lien as a unique stream requiring distinct handling
   115|- [[lien-management-health-insurance]] — health insurance lien as another stream

   298|- **SOL alerts run on closed cases**: Filevine's automated SOL countdown does not account for case status — alerts continue even after a case is declined.

    16|---
    17|
    18|# Filing Litigation as Negotiation Leverage
    19|
    20|> This connection documents how [[case-phase-transitions]] from Negotiation to Litigation is not simply a failure of negotiation but a deliberate strategic choice that interacts with [[statute-of-limitations-monitoring]], [[attorney-review-triggers]], and [[multi-carrier-multi-defendant-cases]] to create leverage for better outcomes.
    21|
    22|## Connection
    23|
    24|Three concepts converge when litigation is used as leverage:
    25|
    26|1. **Low Initial Offer** ([[attorney-review-triggers]]): The BI carrier makes an initial offer that values only a fraction of the treatment (e.g., considering only the ER visit while ignoring extensive follow-up care). The attorney evaluates the offer and determines it is inadequate.
    27|
    28|2. **Pre-emptive Filing** ([[statute-of-limitations-monitoring]] + [[case-phase-transitions]]): Rather than continuing to negotiate from a weak position, the attorney files suit well before the SOL deadline. This signals to the carrier that the firm is serious and the case will not be resolved cheaply.
    29|
    30|3. **Parallel Negotiation** ([[multi-carrier-multi-defendant-cases]]): When multiple carriers are involved, filing suit against one defendant can create leverage with the others. The non-litigating carriers see active litigation and may become more motivated to settle to avoid being drawn in.
    31|
    32|## Evidence
    33|
    34|- In Case D, West Bend's first BI offer was ~$13K, considering only the initial ER visit despite the client having 20+ providers and extensive treatment for concussion, spine, and cervical injuries. The attorney filed suit in Jefferson Circuit Court approximately 1 year post-accident. The litigation then drove discovery, depositions, and defense IMEs over the next 2 years. Meanwhile, the Progressive adjuster (a different carrier on the same case) began proactively calling to discuss settlement. The adjuster noted in a voicemail that they wanted to "talk this one over" — suggesting the existence of active litigation against a co-defendant may have motivated engagement.
    35|
    36|## Insight
    37|
    38|Filing suit is not a last resort — it is a strategic tool. In cases with multiple defendants, it can unlock movement on otherwise stalled negotiations. The cost of litigation (court filing fees, court reporters, preparation time) is an investment in leverage. Cases where the initial offer is dramatically below the treatment value are strong candidates for this strategy.
    39|
    40|## Related
    41|
    42|- [[case-phase-transitions]] — litigation is a phase transition, not just a negotiation failure
    43|- [[attorney-review-triggers]] — the decision to file requires attorney judgment
    44|- [[statute-of-limitations-monitoring]] — filing preserves the claim regardless of negotiation outcome
    45|- [[multi-carrier-multi-defendant-cases]] — litigation against one creates pressure on others
    46|", "total_lines": 45, "file_size": 3282, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"success": true, "diff": "--- a//opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/concepts/wrongful-death-case-patterns.md
+++ b//opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/concepts/wrongful-death-case-patterns.md
@@ -44,6 +44,7 @@
 
 ## Evidence
 
+- In a wrongful death MVA case (DOI 8/2023, KY jurisdiction), the decedent was treated at a university hospital and by EMS but subsequently died. A family member served as primary contact. The case involved a BI claim against a risk management services company and a PIP claim. The firm was communicating through family members (daughter-in-law and husband of the decedent). A large health insurance lien ($89K+) through a union benefits trust was present, requiring calls to the trust's main line and escalation when member status was flagged as "not active" — requiring reinstatement coordination. The firm also retained a collision reconstruction expert. The case involved court filings (circuit and district) with associated filing fees. A lien verification service (Zipliens) was used. The case demonstrates that wrongful death cases from MVAs may still involve substantial hospital treatment and bills (unlike instant-death cases), and that health insurance liens through union/trust benefit plans add another layer of complexity with unique contact and escalation patterns.
 - In a wrongful death case (DOI 2/2022, KY jurisdiction), the decedent was pronounced dead at the scene of a multi-vehicle crash. A surviving family member served as representative and primary contact. The case involved three separate BI carriers (a personal auto carrier, a commercial trucking company's national liabilities office, and a third personal auto carrier), each requiring independent LOR filing and claim setup. Investigation costs exceeded $20K (focus investigation services). Accident reconstruction ($2K) and vocational economics evaluation ($3.2K) were retained. The case was filed in circuit court (Marshall County 23-CI-00073). Court reporter costs across depositions exceeded $1.1K. Additional litigation expenses included multiple defense law firms ($570, $688, $2K, $2.1K). Settlement release negotiations extended over months with back-and-forth emails between the firm and defense counsel. A Medicare question arose during settlement, requiring coordination. The total case timeline exceeded 3 years from DOI with resolution still pending. Pre-settlement funding ($4K) and workers' compensation lien ($171K+) were also present, adding significant lien complexity. A notary service was used to obtain the representative's signature remotely.
 
 ## Agent Guidance

{"success": true, "diff": "--- a//opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/concepts/dog-bite-case-patterns.md
+++ b//opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/concepts/dog-bite-case-patterns.md
@@ -56,6 +56,7 @@
 
 ## Evidence
 
+- In a dog bite/premises liability case (DOI 10/2023, KY jurisdiction), the client was an adult walking his dog when attacked by a neighbor's pit bull that escaped from under a chain-link fence. The case was classified as both "dog_bite" and "premise" liability. The firm identified the dog owner's address, sent LOR via certified mail, and contacted the owner's homeowner's insurer (a major national carrier). The client was treated at an ER/urgent care and a follow-up medical provider. The health insurance lien was minimal ($15). The case moved to litigation with complaint filed in Jefferson Circuit Court against both the dog owner and the property LLC where the animal was housed. Discovery correspondence was exchanged with defense counsel from a major law firm. Depositions were scheduled, and the case generated heavy correspondence between plaintiff and defense counsel over months. The attorney contacted the client to evaluate the case for settlement value. This case confirms that adult dog bite cases follow the same liability investigation pattern as minor cases, and that property owner LLCs can be named as separate defendants in addition to the animal owner — creating multi-defendant litigation complexity.
 - In a dog bite case involving a minor (DOI 8/2022), the firm identified the animal's location (adjacent property), performed an address search to identify residents, and sent certified mail LOR to the identified occupants. The first certified letter was unclaimed and returned; a second letter was signed by a defendant. After 30+ days with no insurance response, the firm texted the defendant directly requesting a photo of their homeowner's insurance card. Insurance was eventually identified as a regional farm bureau carrier. The guardian provided video evidence from a neighbor's home security system showing the animal running loose and attacking the client. The case moved to litigation when settlement appeared insufficient. Depositions of both guardian and minor were scheduled, cancelled by defense counsel, then rescheduled. A jury trial date was ultimately set. The case spanned 3+ years from DOI to ongoing litigation.
 
 ## Agent Guidance
", "files_modified": ["/opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/concepts/dog-bite-case-patterns.md"], "lint": {"status": "skipped", "message": "No linter for .md files"}}

{"success": true, "diff": "--- a//opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/concepts/litigation-process-patterns.md
+++ b//opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/concepts/litigation-process-patterns.md
@@ -79,6 +79,10 @@
 - In a PL case (retail slip-and-fall, McLean County 24-CI-00012), complaint was filed against three corporate defendants (retail chain, property management company, property owner). Defense counsel from a large firm actively engaged in email correspondence about case status. Mediation was conducted but was unsuccessful. The case continued through active litigation with ongoing records collection from 10+ medical providers. Court reporter and mediation expenses were tracked ($400 mediation, $308 court reporting). The multi-defendant PL structure with separate defense representation confirms the pattern that PL cases produce the most litigation-intensive posture.
 - In a wrongful death case (Marshall County 23-CI-00073), the estate representative (surviving family member) was the litigation contact. The case involved three separate BI carriers (two auto carriers + one commercial trucking carrier) and required extensive investigation ($20K+ in investigation costs), accident reconstruction ($2K), and vocational economics evaluation ($3.2K). Court reporter costs across multiple depositions exceeded $1.1K. Settlement release negotiations spanned months with back-and-forth emails. A Medicare question arose during settlement, requiring coordination with defense counsel. This confirms that wrongful death cases in litigation incur substantially higher expert/investigation costs.
 - In an MVA case (Floyd County, IN), complaint was filed as a multi-plaintiff action (companion cases from the same accident). Deposition scheduling involved coordination through court reporting services. The case progressed through discovery to mediation scheduling. Defense counsel correspondence was ongoing through early 2026. The case crossed state lines (KY firm filing in IN court), adding jurisdictional complexity.
+- In an auto accident case (DOI 4/2023, KY, Jefferson Circuit Court), the case entered litigation after BI carrier accepted only partial liability. The case involved 7 medical providers and a Medicare lien. Defense counsel (from a regional PI defense firm) engaged in active discovery correspondence. Depositions were scheduled and defense counsel offered to negotiate on a 50/50 basis. The attorney spoke with the client explaining "issues with case and DC's offer to negotiate 50/50." Multiple outbound call attempts to the client (phone "just rings and gives busy signal") preceded the deposition discussion. This case confirms the pattern that disputed liability is a primary litigation trigger and that defense counsel may propose percentage-based settlement structures during litigation.
+- In an auto accident case (DOI 10/2023, KY, Jefferson Circuit Court), the case entered litigation despite relatively low treatment value. The case had multiple insurance carriers (BI from a state mutual, UM from a national carrier, PIP from the same national carrier). The complaint filing generated standard eFiling notifications (NEF/NCP). The case involved 8 medical providers including chiropractic treatment centers at multiple locations. Defense counsel correspondence extended over months. This confirms that cases with UM claims frequently proceed to litigation when BI carrier coverage is insufficient.
+- In an auto accident case (DOI 9/2021, KY, Jefferson Circuit Court), the case was in active litigation after extensive treatment (10 medical providers, $176K+ in total billing including $141K+ from a single hospital). The case involved a UIM claim alongside the BI claim, with adjuster reassignment observed on the UIM carrier. The case entered litigation after negotiation produced inadequate offers. Discovery was ongoing with defense counsel correspondence. Medicaid lien ($2,452) was confirmed. The case demonstrated the pattern that high-value treatment cases with policy limits pressure tend to enter litigation, and that UIM litigation creates a parallel track with separate adjuster management.
+- In a dog bite/premises liability case (DOI 10/2023, Jefferson Circuit Court), complaint was filed against both the dog owner and the property LLC. Defense counsel from a major national law firm engaged in extensive discovery correspondence spanning months. Depositions were scheduled and settlement evaluation was underway. The case illustrates that dog bite cases naming both animal owner and property entity as defendants create multi-party litigation with sophisticated defense counsel.
 - In a WC estate case (KY DWC claim 202200007), the full WC litigation lifecycle was observed: complaint filing with IROG, initial denial (Motion to Dismiss for "no living person named"), Motion to Substitute Plaintiff, Forms 104/105 and 45-Day Disclosures, BRC hearings (repeatedly rescheduled over 3+ years), defense RFPD, multiple IMEs through Commonwealth IME, ALJ opinion, and WC Board opinion concurring. Total timeline: 5+ years. This confirms the WC administrative proceedings track as fundamentally different from circuit court litigation, with LMS as the filing system.
 - In a multi-defendant auto accident case in litigation (Jefferson Circuit Court, 21-CI-004985, DOI 1/2021), the litigation spanned 4+ years (2021-2025+). Activities included: complaint filing, interrogatory responses to defense counsel, deposition scheduling with defense counsel, State Farm motion to dismiss (AOC 280 Final Submission for Adjudication), certified medical records collection via ChartSwap, government open records requests for police body cam footage (via Axon/Evidence.com), multiple ORRs to LMPD, amended subpoenas (to Verizon and Metro PCS for phone records), Louisville Metro public records requests, forwarding evidence to defense counsel (Dinsmore, Smith & Hoskins), and locating witnesses (other vehicle passengers). This is the most complex litigation case observed, involving multiple defense counsel, government records, and electronic evidence. The 4+ year timeline from DOI to ongoing litigation confirms that multi-defendant cases with uninsured drivers dramatically extend case duration.
 - In Case M (MVA, multi-plaintiff litigation, Floyd County), when four plaintiffs' counsel could not agree on distribution of tendered policy limits, the case moved to litigation. All plaintiffs were joined in one lawsuit. Litigation activity included deposition scheduling via court reporters, extensive correspondence between plaintiff and defense counsel over deposition dates and mediation scheduling, and preparation for mediation. The deposition scheduling required multiple rounds of email coordination spanning weeks (Nov 2025-Feb 2026). This case demonstrates that litigation can be triggered not by low offers or coverage denial but by inter-plaintiff distribution disputes.

While the BI claim was in active litigation (Morgan County Circuit Court 24-CI-00065), the UIM claim remained on a parallel track with separate correspondence to the UIM adjuster. The case then moved to litigation (complaint filed in Jefferson Circuit Court 21-CI-003798) and has remained in litigation for 5+ years. This confirms the pattern that premises liability cases frequently bypass extended negotiation and proceed to litigation. The case also involved a referral to outside counsel (Joel Franklin) during the litigation phase, indicating that PL cases against institutional defendants may require specialist litigation counsel.
- In a wrongful death MVA case (DOI 8/2023, KY), the case progressed from PNC -> File Setup -> Treatment status but with the decedent already deceased, the "treatment" status reflected records collection, not active care. The case then moved toward litigation with collision reconstruction experts retained and court filings in both circuit and district courts. This confirms that wrongful death cases challenge the standard phase model — the treatment phase is repurposed as an investigation/records collection phase.
- In an auto accident case (DOI 4/2023, KY), the phase sequence was PNC -> File Setup -> Treatment -> Litigation. The transition to litigation was driven by disputed liability (defense accepting partial fault). The case spent extended time in treatment with 7 providers before moving to litigation. Defense counsel offered 50/50 negotiation during litigation. This confirms that disputed liability remains a primary litigation trigger distinct from low offers or SOL pressure.
- In an auto accident case (DOI 10/2023, KY), the case progressed PNC -> File Setup -> Treatment -> Demand in Progress -> Litigation. The presence of UM and PIP alongside BI created a three-track insurance structure. The transition to litigation occurred when BI coverage was insufficient and UM was needed. This confirms that UM/UIM coverage insufficiency is a distinct litigation trigger.
- In an auto accident case (DOI 9/2021, KY), the case went directly from File Setup to Negotiation (negotiation phase_change logged 6/2023, ~21 months post-DOI), then to Litigation (phase_change 2/2025). The 21-month negotiation phase before litigation is the longest observed negotiation-to-litigation delay, driven by extensive treatment ($176K+ in bills) and multiple adjuster reassignments. This confirms that high-treatment-value cases with policy limits pressure generate extended negotiation before eventual litigation.
- In an auto accident case (DOI 10/2024, KY), the case progressed rapidly through PNC -> File Setup -> Treatment and into Negotiation phase. One BI carrier settled quickly (KFB), while a second BI carrier remained in active negotiation. This is the first observed case where one of multiple BI carriers settles while the other remains in negotiation — confirming that multi-carrier cases can have split-phase statuses."}

Defense counsel offered 50/50 negotiation during litigation. This confirms that disputed liability remains a primary litigation trigger distinct from low offers or SOL pressure.
+- In an auto accident case (DOI 10/2023, KY), the case progressed PNC -> File Setup -> Treatment -> Demand in Progress -> Litigation. The presence of UM and PIP alongside BI created a three-track insurance structure. The transition to litigation occurred when BI coverage was insufficient and UM was needed. This confirms that UM/UIM coverage insufficiency is a distinct litigation trigger.
+- In an auto accident case (DOI 9/2021, KY), the case went directly from File Setup to Negotiation (negotiation phase_change logged 6/2023, ~21 months post-DOI), then to Litigation (phase_change 2/2025). The 21-month negotiation phase before litigation is the longest observed negotiation-to-litigation delay, driven by extensive treatment ($176K+ in bills) and multiple adjuster reassignments. This confirms that high-treatment-value cases with policy limits pressure generate extended negotiation before eventual litigation.
+- In an auto accident case (DOI 10/2024, KY), the case progressed rapidly through PNC -> File Setup -> Treatment and into Negotiation phase. One BI carrier settled quickly (KFB), while a second BI carrier remained in active negotiation. This is the first observed case where one of multiple BI carriers settles while the other remains in negotiation — confirming that multi-carrier cases can have split-phase statuses.

This confirms that in disputed-liability cases, negotiation may occur within the litigation context rather than following the standard demand-offer-counter sequence.
 - In Case M (MVA, multi-plaintiff), the negotiation phase had a unique twist: the BI carrier tendered policy limits (25/50) within one day of the demand being sent, but settlement could not proceed because the limits had to be split among four injured plaintiffs represented by different firms. The negotiation shifted from firm-vs-insurer to inter-counsel allocation. The attorney emailed all co-plaintiffs' counsel to negotiate distribution. One attorney was slow to provide medical bills, stalling the process for months. The firm updated the client regularly but could only say "we are still waiting." When inter-counsel agreement was not reached, the case moved to litigation. This shows a novel negotiation pattern: the insurer has already paid limits, but the money cannot be distributed without all parties' agreement.
 - In Case R (auto accident, minor injury — airbag rash, DOI 4/2023), a $5,000 demand was sent to the BI carrier. The carrier responded with a $500 "nuisance offer." The firm refused to negotiate further until the defendant had been deposed. After the defendant's deposition (3/2025 — nearly 2 years after the accident), the negotiation was expected to resume. This demonstrates a pattern where the firm deliberately pauses negotiation to build evidentiary leverage through litigation discovery, rather than accepting lowball offers on minor injury cases.
 - In Case S (auto accident, government transit defendant, DOI 9/2021), a BI settlement offer of $15,000 was received from a government risk management entity during active litigation. The firm was simultaneously corresponding with the government entity's attorney about the case. The negotiation during litigation pattern — where offers continue even after complaint filing — is confirmed by this case. Additionally, multiple liens (Medicare, Humana at $1,513, and health insurance) had to be resolved in parallel with the negotiation, complicating the settlement math on a government-defendant case.

# Split Settlement Complicates Multi-Carrier Math

> This connection documents how [[multi-carrier-negotiation]] creates a split-settlement scenario where one carrier resolves while others remain open, interacting with [[negotiation-after-demand]] strategy and [[case-phase-transitions]] phase management.

## Connection

When a PI case involves multiple BI carriers, they may settle at different times — creating a "split settlement" that complicates both ongoing negotiation and case phase management:

1. **Carrier A settles**: The first carrier's BI claim is resolved, producing an initial recovery amount. The case cannot move to "Settlement" phase because other claims remain open.

2. **Carrier B continues negotiating**: The remaining carrier must factor in the prior settlement when evaluating its exposure. The firm must track what has already been recovered vs. what remains outstanding.

3. **Phase ambiguity**: The case is simultaneously in "Settlement" (for one carrier) and "Negotiation" or "Litigation" (for others). Filevine's single-phase status model cannot represent this multi-track state.

4. **Lien allocation**: Health insurance liens and other subrogation interests must be allocated across the split settlements, adding complexity to the final disbursement.

## Evidence

- In an auto accident case (DOI 10/2024, KY), one of two BI carriers (a state mutual) settled after demand while the second carrier (a regional insurer) remained in active negotiation. The SOL countdown (180 days) was running on the open claim. The case was in "negotiation" status despite having a partial settlement in place. Three adjusters were active across BI and PIP tracks.
- In a multi-plaintiff auto accident case (DOI 8/2023, KY/IN), multiple BI carriers were involved. One carrier settled (Liberty Mutual) and another settled (State Farm), while a third carrier remained open. The settled amounts from early carriers had to be factored into the ongoing negotiation and litigation with the remaining carrier.

## Related

- [[multi-carrier-negotiation]] — the multi-carrier structure that creates split settlement scenarios
- [[negotiation-after-demand]] — ongoing negotiation with remaining carriers after partial settlement
- [[case-phase-transitions]] — phase management challenges when carriers resolve at different times
"}

The spouse handled all communications, called for updates repeatedly, completed probate inventory, and relayed information from the home health provider that corrected a records request error (wrong provider name). The spouse showed classic frustration escalation — calling for updates, being told the attorney would call back, then calling again when no callback came. This is a distinct variant of the family-as-contact pattern: when the client has died, the family member is not just a proxy but the actual party (as estate representative), adding both legal and emotional complexity.

4. `lien-management-health-insurance` — 8->11 (added union trust lien $89K+, Medicaid MCO multi-entity liens, Medicaid lien vs total billing ratio)
5. `uim-claim-management` — 3->5, confidence low->medium (added UM three-adjuster pattern, UIM adjuster reassignment)
6. `adjuster-reassignment-patterns` — 1->3 (added UIM adjuster reassignment, 7-adjuster-per-case record)
7. `case-phase-transitions` — 17->22 (added wrongful death phase repurposing, disputed liability trigger confirmation, UM coverage trigger, 21-month negotiation delay, split-settlement phase ambiguity)
8. `unreachable-client-patterns` — 4->6 (added ring/busy signal pattern, Zoom deposition technology unreachability)
9. `negotiation-after-demand` — 4->6 (added split-settlement pattern, in-litigation 50/50 negotiation)
10. `multi-carrier-negotiation` — 1->3 (added BI+UM+PIP 3-track coordination, split-settlement math)
11. `client-communication-through-family` — 8->9 (added wrongful death family-as-actual-client pattern)

### Confidence Upgrades
- `uim-claim-management`: low -> medium (5 cases)

### Key New Patterns Identified
- **Union/multi-employer trust benefit plan liens are the largest health insurance liens observed**: A TeamCare union trust lien of $89K+ dwarfs typical private health insurer liens, and trust plan administrative issues (inactive member status) create unique delays
- **Split settlements create multi-track phase ambiguity**: When one BI carrier settles while another remains open, the case is simultaneously resolved and unresolved — Filevine's single-phase model cannot represent this state
- **Technology unreachability during depositions is a new pattern**: Clients willing but technically unable to participate in Zoom depositions require real-time text troubleshooting
- **Ring/busy signal is a distinct unreachability type**: Unlike voicemail-full or disconnected, indefinite ring/busy suggests line issues or call blocking
- **50/50 negotiation proposals occur during litigation**: Defense counsel may propose percentage-based settlement structures as a litigation resolution strategy
- **7 adjusters per case is the new maximum observed**: Multi-track cases (PIP + BI + UM) with parent/subsidiary carrier structures generate the most adjuster churn
- **Wrongful death MVAs may still have extensive hospital treatment**: Unlike instant-death cases, some wrongful death cases involve significant hospital bills and treatment records
- **Dog bite cases naming property LLCs as co-defendants attract sophisticated defense counsel**: Adding the property entity as a defendant can result in major national law firms on the defense side

### Gaps Partially Addressed
- **UIM claim processing**: Now 5 cases with UIM evidence; confidence upgraded to medium
- **Dog bite litigation**: Second case adds adult victim and property LLC defendant pattern
- **Wrongful death variants**: Second case shows MVA-with-treatment wrongful death (vs instant death)
- **Multi-carrier coordination**: Split-settlement pattern now documented

### Remaining Gaps
- Settlement disbursement: Still no cases with completed final disbursement
- Union trust lien negotiation: Unknown whether union trust liens can be negotiated down
- Zoom deposition protocol: Need formal checklist for technology preparation
- Dog bite case valuations: Still no settlement data for comparison
- Split-settlement allocation methodology: How to allocate liens across partial settlements"}
